pages.title: Recollections on étale morphisms
pages.html:
1 Recollections on étale morphisms

We are going to review here the properties of étale morphisms (developed in I) that will be of use to us, taking this opportunity to eliminate from the theory the superfluous Noetherian hypotheses. The reader will note that even if one is interested only in Noetherian schemes, the technique of descent leads one to introduce non-Noetherian schemes (such as \(\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{A}\otimes_{A}\widehat{A})\), where \(A\) is a Noetherian local ring), and in order to be able to apply the language of fibred categories, it is important to define notions such as étale morphism etc., without introducing into them any Noetherian restriction. The reader who would be reluctant to verify or to admit that the statements below are true without Noetherian hypotheses may content himself with admitting them under the Noetherian hypotheses of I, provided he introduces these same Noetherian hypotheses into the statements of the following sections, and uses the definition 1 below for the non-Noetherian schemes that arise in the arguments.

Definition 1

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of preschemes, and \(x\) a point of \(X\). One says that \(f\) is étale at \(x\), or that \(X\) is étale over \(S\) at \(x\), if there exists an affine open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s=f(x)\), an affine open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(x\) above \(U\), an affine Noetherian scheme \(U_{0}\), an étale (I) and affine \(U_{0}\)-scheme \(V_{0}\), a morphism \(U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U_{0}\), and a \(U\)-isomorphism

\[ V\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} V_{0}\times_{U_{0}}U. \]

One will note that when \(S\) is locally Noetherian, this terminology coincides with that of loc. cit. One will say likewise that \(f\) is étale, or that \(X\) is étale over \(S\), if \(f\) is étale at every point \(x\) of \(X\). With these definitions, the propositions below reduce without difficulty to the Noetherian case, where they are proved in I 4, 5, 7. For details, the reader may consult EGA IV1.

Remarks 2

If \(f\) is étale at \(x\), then \(f\) is “of finite presentation at \(x\)” (VIII 5), the local ring of \(x\) in the fibre \(f^{-1}(s)\) is a finite separable extension of \(\mathit{k}(s)\), and finally \(f\) is flat at \(x\). One can show that the converse is true, hence that definition 1 is the same as in the case where \(S\) is locally Noetherian, except that one must replace the condition “of finite type at \(x\)” by “of finite presentation at \(x\)”. Since this result is of delicate proof, we did not wish here to give this definition of the notion of étale morphism, which does not lend itself directly to the proof of the properties that are to follow.

Let us note first that one has trivially:

Proposition 3

If \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is étale, then every morphism \(f'\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) deduced from it by base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is likewise étale.

One may therefore say that the étale morphisms form a fibred subcategory of the category of arrows in \(\mathbf{Sch}\) (cf. VI 11 a)). The object of the present exposé is the study of the exactness properties of this category fibred over \(\mathbf{Sch}\).

Proposition 4

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of preschemes. For it to be an open immersion, it is necessary and sufficient that it be étale and radicial.

Cf. I 1. One concludes from this that if \(X\) is étale over \(S\), every section of \(X\) over \(S\) is an open immersion, hence, using again 4, one finds:

Corollary 5

Let \(X\) be an étale \(S\)-prescheme. Then there is a one-to-one correspondence between the set of sections of \(X\) over \(S\), and the set of open parts \(\Gamma\) of \(X\) such that the morphism \(\Gamma \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) induced by the structural morphism is radicial and surjective.

If moreover \(X\) is separated over \(S\), \(\Gamma\) will be a part of \(X\) at once open and closed, but this is of no importance. — Making an obvious base change, one can put 5 in the apparently more general form:

Corollary 6

Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two \(S\)-preschemes, \(Y\) being étale over \(S\). Then the map \(f \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \Gamma_f\) which associates to every \(S\)-morphism \(f\) of \(X\) into \(Y\) the part of \(X\times_S Y\) underlying the graph of \(f\), is a bijection of \(\operatorname{Hom}_S(X,Y)\) onto the set of open parts \(\Gamma\) of \(X\times_S Y\) such that the morphism \(\Gamma\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) induced by \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) is radicial and surjective.

Proposition 7

Let \(S_0\) be the subprescheme of \(S\) defined by a quasi-coherent Nil-ideal, i.e. such that \(S_0\) has the same underlying set as \(S\). Then the functor \(X \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X\times_S S_0\) from the category of preschemes étale over \(S\) into the category of preschemes étale over \(S_0\), is an equivalence of categories.

The fact that this functor is fully faithful is an immediate consequence of 6. The fact that it is essentially surjective is contained in I 3. One will note that in the preceding equivalence, \(X\) is of finite type i.e. quasi-finite over \(S\), (resp. finite i.e. an étale covering of \(S\)), if and only if \(X_0\) satisfies the analogous condition over \(S_0\); the same remark for the condition of separation. These facts are immediate, and also contained in 4 further below.

Corollary 8

Let \(A\) be a complete Noetherian local ring with residue field \(k\). Then the functor \(B \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} B\otimes_A k\) is an equivalence of the category of finite and étale algebras over \(A\), with the category of finite and étale algebras over \(k\), (i.e. composed of a finite number of finite separable extensions of \(k\)).

Proposition 9

For \(X\) to be an étale covering of \(S\) i.e. finite and étale over \(S\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(X\) be \(S\)-isomorphic to the spectrum of an Algebra \(\mathscr{A}\) over \(S\), which is a locally free Module of finite type, and such that for every \(s \in S\), \(\mathscr{A}_s\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_s}\mathit{k}(s)\) is a separable algebra over \(\mathit{k}(s)\), hence in the present case a direct composite of finite separable extensions of \(\mathit{k}(s)\).

Finally the following result is of a less elementary nature, being the conjunction of I 4 and of the existence theorem for sheaves in algebraic geometry, (EGA III 5; cf. also [1] th. 3).

Theorem 10

Let \(S\) be the spectrum of a complete Noetherian local ring, \(X\) a proper \(S\)-scheme, \(X_0\) the fibre of \(X\) at the closed point of \(S\), (so that \(X_0\) is a closed subscheme of \(X\)). Then the restriction functor \(X' \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X'\times_X X_0\) is an equivalence of the category of étale coverings of \(X\) with the category of étale coverings of \(X_0\).

pages.title: Submersive and universally submersive morphisms
pages.html:
2 Submersive and universally submersive morphisms

Definition 1

A morphism \(g\colon S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) of preschemes is said to be submersive if it is surjective, and makes \(S\) a topological quotient space of \(S'\) (i.e. a subset \(U\) of \(S\) such that \(f^{-1}(U)\) is open, is open). One says that \(f\) is universally submersive if for every morphism \(T \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), the morphism \(f'\colon T'=S'\times_S T \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) deduced from \(f\) by base change is submersive.

It is immediate that the composite of two submersive morphisms (resp. universally submersive) is submersive (resp. universally submersive), and that a base change in a universally submersive morphism gives a universally submersive morphism (given that one does what is required for this). If \(fg\) is submersive (resp. universally submersive), then so is \(f\).

Examples 2

a) A surjective morphism which is open, or closed, is submersive, hence a universally closed or universally open surjective morphism is universally submersive. For example a proper surjective morphism is universally submersive. On the other hand a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism is universally submersive (VIII 3). These will be the two most important cases for us.

One can apply to a submersive or universally submersive morphism \(g\colon S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) the arguments of VIII 3, one finds in particular:

Proposition 3

Suppose \(g\colon S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) submersive. Then the following diagram of maps is exact:

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{Ouv} (S)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Ouv} (S')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathrm{Ouv}(S''),}} \]

where \(S'' = S'\times_S S'\), and where \(\operatorname{Ouv}(X)\) denotes the set of open subsets of the prescheme \(X\).

Proposition 4

Let \(g\colon S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a universally submersive morphism, \(f\colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) an \(S\)-morphism, and \(f'\colon X' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\) the \(S'\)-morphism deduced from it by base change. In order for \(f\) to be open (resp. closed), it suffices that \(f'\) be so. In order for \(f\) to be universally open, resp. universally closed, resp. separated, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be so. If moreover \(g\) is quasi-compact, and \(f\) locally of finite type, then in order for \(f\) to be proper, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be so.

For this last point, one notes that if \(f'\) is proper hence quasi-compact then \(f\) is quasi-compact (VIII 3) hence of finite type since it is locally of finite type. On the other hand it is separated and universally closed by what precedes, hence it is proper.

Proposition 5

Let \(S'\) be a prescheme of finite type over the spectrum \(S\) of a complete Noetherian local ring, suppose that the fibre of the closed point \(s\) of \(S\) is finite, so that the local rings in \(S'\) of the points \(s'\) of this fibre are finite over \(A=\mathscr{O}_s\). Let \(S''\) be the scheme sum of the spectra of the \(\mathscr{O}_{S',s'}\) in question, considered as a finite \(S\)-scheme. In order for \(g\colon S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) to be universally submersive, it is necessary and sufficient that the structural morphism \(S''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) be surjective.

Since there is a natural \(S\)-morphism \(S'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\), and since a finite surjective morphism is universally submersive by 2, the stated condition is sufficient. Let us show then that if \(S''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) is not surjective, then \(g\) is not universally submersive. Indeed, let \(t\) be a point of \(S\) which is not in the image of \(S''\); there then exists an \(S\)-scheme \(T\), spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, whose image in \(S\) is \(\{s,t\}\). Note that the image of \(S''\) in \(S'\) is open, since the morphism \(S'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) is a local isomorphism, and on the other hand this image contains \(S'_s\), and does not meet \(S'_t\). It follows that the inverse image of the latter in \(T'=S'\times_S T\) is open, and identical to the inverse image of the closed point of \(T\). This shows that \(T'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) is not submersive, hence \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is not universally submersive.

Remark 6

Using the criterion IV 3 for a constructible subset of a Noetherian space to be open, one finds easily the following valuative criterion for a morphism \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) of finite type, with \(S\) locally Noetherian, to be universally submersive: it is necessary and sufficient that for every \(S\)-scheme \(T\), spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, setting \(T'=S'\times_S T\), the inverse image in \(T'\) of the closed point of \(T\) be non-open.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1

A morphism \(g\colon S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) of preschemes is said to be submersive if it is surjective, and makes \(S\) a topological quotient space of \(S'\) (i.e. a subset \(U\) of \(S\) such that \(f^{-1}(U)\) is open, is open). blocks.html:
Examples 2

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3

Suppose \(g\colon S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) submersive. Then the following diagram of maps is exact:

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{Ouv} (S)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Ouv} (S')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathrm{Ouv}(S''),}} \]

where \(S'' = S'\times_S S'\), and where \(\operatorname{Ouv}(X)\) denotes the set of open subsets of the prescheme \(X\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4

Let \(g\colon S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a universally submersive morphism, \(f\colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) an \(S\)-morphism, and \(f'\colon X' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\) the \(S'\)-morphism deduced from it by base change. blocks.html:
Remark 6

pages.title: Descent of morphisms of étale preschemes
pages.html:
3 Descent of morphisms of étale preschemes

Proposition 1

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a surjective morphism of preschemes, \(X\) and \(Y\) two preschemes over \(S\), \(X'\), \(Y'\) their inverse images over \(S'\). If \(Y\) is unramified over \(S\), then the canonical mapping

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_S(X,Y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{S'}(X',Y') \]

is injective.

Indeed, by virtue of 6, an \(S\)-morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is known when one knows the underlying set of its graph \(\Gamma\), which is a part of \(Z=X\times_SY\). Since

\[ Z'=Z\times_SS'=X'\times_{S'}Y' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z \]

is surjective, (since \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is), this part \(\Gamma\) is known when one knows its inverse image in \(X'\times_{S'}Y'\), which is none other than the underlying set of the graph of \(f'\). Whence the conclusion.

A part \(\Gamma\) of \(Z\) is the graph of an \(S\)-morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) if and only if it is open, and if the morphism induced by \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) from \(\Gamma\) to \(Y\) is radicial and surjective cf. 1. When the first property is verified, the second holds if and only if the inverse image \(\Gamma'\) of \(\Gamma\) in \(Z'\) satisfies the same condition VIII 1. If finally one knows that \(Z'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) is submersive, which will be the case in particular if \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is universally submersive, then \(\Gamma\) is open if and only if \(\Gamma'\) is. Thus, the set \(\operatorname{Hom}_S(X,Y)\) is then in one-to-one correspondence with the set of open parts \(\Gamma'\) of \(Z'\) such that the projection morphism \(\mathrm{pr}_1\colon Z'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) is radicial and surjective, (i.e. corresponding to an \(S'\)-morphism \(f'\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\)), and which are saturated for the equivalence relation defined by \(Z'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\), i.e. whose two inverse images in \(Z''=Z'\times_Z Z'=Z\times_S S''\) (where \(S''=S'\times_S S'\)), by one and the other projection, are equal. Now these latter are the graphs of the two \(S''\)-morphisms \(X''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y''\) deduced from \(f'\) by base change, by one and the other projection \(S''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\). One has thus obtained:

Proposition 2

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a universally submersive morphism of preschemes, \(S''=S'\times_S S'\), \(X\) and \(Y\) two \(S\)-preschemes, \(X'\) and \(Y'\) their inverse images over \(S'\), and \(X''\), \(Y''\) their inverse images over \(S''\). If \(Y\) is étale over \(S\) the following canonical diagram of mappings is exact:

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathrm{Hom}_S(X,Y)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{Hom}_{S'}(X',Y')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{{\mathrm{Hom}_{S''}(X'',Y'')}.}} \]

Taking \(X\) and \(Y\) étale over \(S\), one finds the following statement, which moreover gives back 2 (even by restricting to \(X=S\), in which case one can indeed always reduce to this in 2, by the base change \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\));

Corollary 3

A universally submersive morphism of preschemes is a morphism of descent for the fibred category of preschemes étale over others.

I do not know, moreover, whether it is necessarily a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category in question, even by making the further hypothesis that \(S\) is Noetherian and \(g\) of finite type, and by restricting to étale coverings. We shall nevertheless give in the following number useful criteria of effectivity.

Corollary 4

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a universally submersive morphism, whose fibres \(g^{-1}(s)\) are “geometrically connected”, i.e. for every extension \(K/\mathit{k}(s)\), \(g^{-1}(s)\otimes_{\mathit{k}(s)} K\) is connected. Then \(S'\) is connected if \(S\) is. The functor from the category of preschemes étale over \(S\) to the category of preschemes étale over \(S'\) defined by \(g\) is fully faithful.

A part of \(S'\) which is both open and closed is saturated for the set-theoretic equivalence relation defined by \(g\), since the fibres are connected, hence is the inverse image of a part of \(S\), which is necessarily open and closed since \(g\) is submersive. If therefore \(S\) is connected, \(S'\) is. This also implies the following result: the composite \(fg\) of two morphisms with universally connected fibres, \(f\) being universally submersive, has universally connected fibres; if \(S_1'\) and \(S_2'\) over \(S\) have universally connected fibres, the same holds for \(S_1'\times_S S_2'\). In particular, under the conditions of 4, \(S''\) has universally connected fibres over \(S\). Let then \(X\) and \(Y\) be étale over \(S\), and let \(u'\) be an \(S'\)-morphism from \(X'\) to \(Y'\), let us prove that it is compatible with the descent data (which entails the desired conclusion thanks to 3). Now let \(u_1''\) and \(u_2''\) be the two \(S''\)-morphisms \(X''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y''\) deduced from \(u'\). The sub-prescheme of \(S''\) of the coincidences of \(u_1''\) and \(u_2''\) is an induced open sub-prescheme, closed fibre by fibre, as the inverse image of the diagonal prescheme of \(Y''\) over \(S''\)1. It is therefore the inverse image of a part of \(S\). Since it contains the diagonal in \(S''\), it is identical with \(S''\), whence \(u_1''=u_2''\) q.e.d.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a surjective morphism of preschemes, \(X\) and \(Y\) two preschemes over \(S\), \(X'\), \(Y'\) their inverse images over \(S'\). If \(Y\) is unramified over \(S\), then the canonical mapping

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_S(X,Y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{S'}(X',Y') \]

is injective.

Indeed, by virtue of 6, an \(S\)-morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is known when one knows the underlying set of its graph \(\Gamma\), which is a part of \(Z=X\times_SY\). Since
\[ Z'=Z\times_SS'=X'\times_{S'}Y' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z \]

is surjective, (since \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is), this part \(\Gamma\) is known when one knows its inverse image in \(X'\times_{S'}Y'\), which is none other than the underlying set of the graph of \(f'\). Whence the conclusion.

A part \(\Gamma\) of \(Z\) is the graph of an \(S\)-morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) if and only if it is open, and if the morphism induced by \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) from \(\Gamma\) to \(Y\) is radicial and surjective cf. 1. When the first property is verified, the second holds if and only if the inverse image \(\Gamma'\) of \(\Gamma\) in \(Z'\) satisfies the same condition VIII 1. If finally one knows that \(Z'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) is submersive, which will be the case in particular if \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is universally submersive, then \(\Gamma\) is open if and only if \(\Gamma'\) is. Thus, the set \(\operatorname{Hom}_S(X,Y)\) is then in one-to-one correspondence with the set of open parts \(\Gamma'\) of \(Z'\) such that the projection morphism \(\mathrm{pr}_1\colon Z'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) is radicial and surjective, (i.e. corresponding to an \(S'\)-morphism \(f'\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\)), and which are saturated for the equivalence relation defined by \(Z'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\), i.e. whose two inverse images in \(Z''=Z'\times_Z Z'=Z\times_S S''\) (where \(S''=S'\times_S S'\)), by one and the other projection, are equal. Now these latter are the graphs of the two \(S''\)-morphisms \(X''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y''\) deduced from \(f'\) by base change, by one and the other projection \(S''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\). One has thus obtained:

pages.html:
4 Descent of étale preschemes: criteria of effectivity

.7cm

Proposition 1

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism. Then \(g\) is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of étale preschemes, separated and of finite type over others.

It is indeed a morphism of descent for the fibred category in question, by virtue of 3 or of VIII 2, as one prefers. It remains to show that if \(X'\) is étale, separated and of finite type over \(S'\), and equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), this last is effective in the fibred category in question. Now one sees easily that if \(X\) is a prescheme over \(S\), then it is étale over \(S\) if and only if it is étale over \(S'\) (by virtue of definition 1 and of loc. cit. 6). Hence it is étale, separated and of finite type over \(S\) if and only if \(X'\) is so over \(S'\), cf. for example 4. Hence it suffices to make sure of the effectivity of the descent datum on \(X\) for the fibred category of the arrows of \(\mathbf{Sch}\). Now \(X'\) is quasi-affine over \(S'\) by virtue of VIII 2 and 6. One may then conclude by using VIII 9. The reader will note moreover that the proof demands less if one restricts oneself to preschemes étale and finite over others, for one may then invoke directly VIII 1.

Corollary 2

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a universally submersive morphism, \(X'\) a \(S'\)-prescheme étale, separated and of finite type, equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\), \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism, \(S_1'\) and \(X_1'\) deduced from \(S'\) and \(X'\) by the base change, so that \(S_1'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_1\) is universally submersive, \(X_1'\) is étale, separated and of finite type over \(S_1'\), and equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g_1\colon S_1'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_1\). For the descent datum on \(X'\) to be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that the descent datum on \(X_1'\) be so.

This results from the theory of descent in categories [D], taking into account 1 and 3.

One proves in an analogous fashion:

Corollary 3

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a universally submersive morphism, \(X'\) a \(S'\)-prescheme étale equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\), \((S_i)\) a covering of \(S\) by open sets. For the descent datum to be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that for every \(i\), the corresponding descent datum on \(X_i'=X\times_S S_i\), relative to the morphism \(g_i\colon S_i'=S'\times_S S_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_i\), be so.

This last result leads to the isolation of a local criterion of effectivity:

.3cm

Proposition 4

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of finite presentation VIII 6 and universally submersive, \(X'\) a prescheme étale and of finite presentation over \(S'\), equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\), and finally \(a\) a point of \(S\). For there to exist an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(a\), such that the corresponding descent datum on \(X_U'=X'\times_S U\) relative to the morphism

\[ g_U\colon S_U'=S'\times_S U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_U=U \]

be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that the corresponding descent datum on \(X_a'=X'\times_S\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_a)\), relative to the morphism

\[ g_a\colon S_a'=S'\times_S\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_a=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_a), \]

be effective.

The necessity being trivial, let us show the sufficiency. One thus has at one's disposal an étale prescheme of finite type \(X_a\) over \(S_a\), and an isomorphism

\[ \tag{*} {X_a'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X_a\times_{S_a}S_a'} \]

compatible with the descent data. In accordance with an easy general sorites on preschemes defined over a direct limit of rings (here the rings \(A_f\), where \(A\) is the ring of an affine open neighbourhood of \(a\), and where \(f\) runs through the elements of \(A\) that are not in the prime ideal corresponding to \(a\)), one may find an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(a\), an étale prescheme of finite type \(X_U\) over \(U=S_U\), and an \(S_a\)-isomorphism \(X_a\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X_U\times_{S_U}S_a\). Moreover, taking \(U\) small enough, one may then suppose that the isomorphism (*) comes from an isomorphism:

\[ X_U'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X_U\times_{S_U} S_U'; \]

this last might fail to be compatible with the descent data, however on condition of shrinking \(U\), it will be compatible with the descent data. This completes the proof.

Corollary 5

Under the conditions of 4, for the descent datum on \(X'\) to be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that for every \(a\in S\), the corresponding descent datum on \(X_a'\), relative to the morphism \(S_a'=S'\times_S\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_a)\), be so. When \(S\) is locally Noetherian, and \(X'\) separated over \(S'\), one may in the preceding criterion also replace \(\mathscr{O}_a\) by its completion.

The first assertion results from 4 and 3, the second is then a consequence of 2. Using again 2 and the fact that for every local Noetherian ring \(A\), one may find a complete local Noetherian ring \(B\), and a local homomorphism \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\), such that \(B\) is flat over \(A\) and that \(B/\mathfrak{m}B\) is a given extension of the residue field \(k=A/\mathfrak{m}\) of \(A\), one finds:

Corollary 6

Under the conditions of 4, suppose moreover \(X'\) separated over \(S'\), and \(S\) locally Noetherian. For the descent datum on \(X'\) to be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that for every prescheme \(S_1\) over \(S\), spectrum of a complete local ring with algebraically closed residue field, the corresponding descent datum on \(X_1'=X'\times_S S_1\), relative to the morphism \(g_1\colon S_1'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_1\), be effective.

Theorem 7

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a finite and surjective morphism, and of finite presentation (this last hypothesis being a consequence of the others if \(S\) is locally Noetherian)1. Then \(g\) is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of preschemes étale, separated, of finite type over others.

It must be shown that if \(X'\) is étale, separated, of finite type over \(S'\), and equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\), then this datum is effective. Using 3, one reduces easily to the case where \(S\) is Noetherian. Thanks to 5, one may thus suppose that \(S\) is the spectrum of a local Noetherian ring, a fortiori that

\[ \dim S=n<+\infty. \]

One then argues by induction on \(\dim S=n\), the assertion being trivial for \(n<0\). Suppose then \(n\geqslant 0\) and the theorem proved for the dimensions \(n'<n\). By virtue of 6 one is reduced to the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of a complete local ring, hence \(S'\) is a finite union of spectra of complete local rings. One thus has

\[ X'=X_1'\sqcup X_2' \]

where \(X_1'\) is finite over \(S'\), and where \(X_2'\) has no point above any of the closed points of \(S'\). Consider the morphisms

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{q_1,q_2\colon X''}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{X'}} \]

corresponding to the descent datum, compatible with \(\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{p_1,p_2\colon S''}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{S'}}\). One sees at once that

\[ X''=q_i^{-1}(X_1')\sqcup q_i^{-1}(X_2')\quad i=1,2 \]

is the analogous canonical decomposition of \(X''\) over \(S''\), which implies \(q_1^{-1}(X_1')= q_2^{-1}(X_1')\) and consequently \(X_1'\) and \(X_2'\) are endowed with induced descent data. Now let \(T\) be the open subset of \(S\) complementary to its closed point, so that \(T'=S'\times_S T\) is the part of \(S'\) complementary to the set of closed points, and \(X_2'\), which lies entirely above \(T'\), is endowed with a descent datum relative to the morphism \(T'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) induced by \(g\). Since the latter is finite surjective, and since \(\dim T<\dim S=n\), this descent datum is effective by the induction hypothesis. We thus see that it suffices to prove that the descent datum on \(X_1'\) is effective, so we may now assume \(X'\) étale and finite over \(S'\). (N.B. the argument by induction is unnecessary if one restricts to étale coverings in statement 7). Let then \(S_0\) be the spectrum of the residue field of \(A\), let \(S_0'=S'\times_S S_0\) and let us define likewise \(S_0''\), \(S_0'''\) from the fibred squares and cubes \(S''\) and \(S'''\) of \(S'\) over \(S\). By virtue of 8, the morphisms \(S_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), \(S_0'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\), etc. induce equivalences for the categories of étale coverings of \(S\) and \(S_0\) on the one hand, \(S'\) and \(S_0'\) on the other hand, etc. By the sorites of descent theory in categories [D], it follows that in order for \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) to be a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of étale coverings, it is necessary and sufficient that this be so for \(g_0\colon S_0'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_0\). But this is indeed the case, as a particular case of 1, for example. This completes the proof.

Corollary 8

The conclusion of 7 remains valid if one assumes only that \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is universally submersive, of finite type and quasi-finite, provided one assumes \(S\) locally Noetherian.

By virtue of 6, we may indeed assume that \(S\) is the spectrum of a complete Noetherian local ring. Then by virtue of 5, there exists a finite surjective morphism \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), and an \(S\)-morphism \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\). Since \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism of universal strict descent for the fibred category considered, by virtue of 7, and since \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism of universal descent for the said category, 8 follows from the general sorites [D].

Corollary 9

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of finite type, surjective and universally open, with \(S\) locally Noetherian. Then \(g\) is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of preschemes étale, separated and of finite type over others.

Proceeding as in 7, we are reduced to the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of a complete Noetherian local ring \(A\). Let \(A_1\) be a finite algebra over \(A\), with spectrum \(S_1\), such that \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is finite and surjective, hence a morphism of universal effective descent for the fibred category considered, thanks to 7. It then follows from the general theorems [D] that \(g\) is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category considered if and only if the corresponding morphism \(g_1\colon S_1'=S'\times_S S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_1\) is. Since the latter satisfies the same hypotheses as \(g\), we are reduced to proving 9 for \(S_1\) instead of \(S\). Taking first for \(A_1\) the direct composite of the \(A/\mathfrak{p}_i\), for the minimal prime ideals \(\mathfrak{p}_i\) of \(A\), we are reduced to the case where \(A\) is integral. One then shows2 that there exists an integral subscheme \(S_1\) of \(S'\), quasi-finite over \(S\) and dominating \(S\), passing through a point of the fibre of \(S'\) at the closed point \(y\) of \(S\) (thanks to the fact that \(S'\) is universally open of finite type over \(S\) local Noetherian integral, and \(S_y'\neq\emptyset\)). Since \(A\) is complete, \(S_1\) is finite over \(S\), and since it dominates \(S\), the morphism \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is surjective. Replacing once more \(S\) by \(S_1\), we are reduced to the case where \(S'\) has a section over \(S\), where the statement is trivial.

Theorem 10

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a finite radicial surjective morphism, of finite presentation (this last condition being superfluous if \(S\) is locally Noetherian3). Then the inverse image functor induces an equivalence of the category of preschemes étale over \(S\) with the category of preschemes étale over \(S'\).

Since the diagonal morphisms of \(S'\) into \(S'\times_S S'\) and \(S'\times_S S'\times_S S'\) are surjective immersions, hence induce by virtue of 9 equivalences of the categories of preschemes étale over \(S'\times_S S'\) resp. \(S'\times_S S'\times_S S'\) with the category of preschemes étale over \(S'\), it follows from the sorites of descent [D] that every \(X'\) étale over \(S'\) is endowed with a descent datum, and with only one, relative to \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). Hence 3 implies that the inverse image functor by \(g\), from the category of preschemes étale over \(S\) into the category of preschemes étale over \(S'\), is fully faithful. It remains to show that it is essentially surjective, i.e. that every \(X'\) étale over \(S'\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of an \(X\) étale over \(S\). The question being obviously local on \(S\) and on \(X'\), we may assume \(S\), \(S'\), \(X'\) affine. But then \(X'\) is separated of finite type over \(S'\), and we may apply the effectivity criterion 7.

Corollary 11

Conclusion 9 remains valid on replacing the hypothesis on \(g\) by: \(g\) is faithfully flat, quasi-compact and radicial.

Same proof, invoking 1 instead of 7.

One will note that the proof of 7 is “elementary” in that it does not use the theorems of finiteness and of comparison for proper morphisms (EGA III 3, 4, 5). This is no longer the case for the following result:

Theorem 12

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper, surjective morphism, of finite presentation (this last hypothesis being a consequence of the first if \(S\) is locally Noetherian). Then \(g\) is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of étale coverings of preschemes.

By virtue of 3 and 2, we are reduced to proving that for every étale covering \(X'\) over \(S'\), equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), this descent datum is effective. Using 3, we are easily reduced to the case where \(S\) is Noetherian, and using 6, we may therefore suppose that \(S\) is the spectrum of a complete Noetherian local ring \(A\). Let us introduce \(S''\) and \(S'''\) as usual, let \(S_0\) be the spectrum of the residue field of \(A\), and let \(S_0'\), \(S_0''\), \(S_0'''\) be deduced from \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\) by the base change \(S_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), i.e. the fibres of \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\) at the closed point of \(S\). By 10, the morphisms \(S_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), \(S_0'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\), etc. induce equivalences of the category of étale coverings on the target scheme with the category of étale coverings on the source scheme. Consequently, \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a strict descent morphism for the fibred category of étale coverings of preschemes if and only if \(g_0\colon S_0'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_0\) is, which is indeed the case by virtue of 1. This completes the proof of 12. (In this argument, we needed of 10 only the fact that the functor considered in 10 is fully faithful, which does not use the existence theorem for coherent sheaves in algebraic geometry.)

blocks.html:
Proposition 4

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of finite presentation VIII 6 and universally submersive, \(X'\) a prescheme étale and of finite presentation over \(S'\), equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\), and finally \(a\) a point of \(S\). For there to exist an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(a\), such that the corresponding descent datum on \(X_U'=X'\times_S U\) relative to the morphism

\[ g_U\colon S_U'=S'\times_S U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_U=U \]

be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that the corresponding descent datum on \(X_a'=X'\times_S\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_a)\), relative to the morphism

\[ g_a\colon S_a'=S'\times_S\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_a=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_a), \]

be effective.
The necessity being trivial, let us show the sufficiency. One thus has at one's disposal an étale prescheme of finite type \(X_a\) over \(S_a\), and an isomorphism
\[ \tag{*} {X_a'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X_a\times_{S_a}S_a'} \]

compatible with the descent data. In accordance with an easy general sorites on preschemes defined over a direct limit of rings (here the rings \(A_f\), where \(A\) is the ring of an affine open neighbourhood of \(a\), and where \(f\) runs through the elements of \(A\) that are not in the prime ideal corresponding to \(a\)), one may find an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(a\), an étale prescheme of finite type \(X_U\) over \(U=S_U\), and an \(S_a\)-isomorphism \(X_a\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X_U\times_{S_U}S_a\). Moreover, taking \(U\) small enough, one may then suppose that the isomorphism (*) comes from an isomorphism:
\[ X_U'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X_U\times_{S_U} S_U'; \]
this last might fail to be compatible with the descent data, however on condition of shrinking \(U\), it will be compatible with the descent data. This completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5

Under the conditions of 4, for the descent datum on \(X'\) to be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that for every \(a\in S\), the corresponding descent datum on \(X_a'\), relative to the morphism \(S_a'=S'\times_S\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_a)\), be so. When \(S\) is locally Noetherian, and \(X'\) separated over \(S'\), one may in the preceding criterion also replace \(\mathscr{O}_a\) by its completion.

The first assertion results from 4 and 3, the second is then a consequence of 2. Using again 2 and the fact that for every local Noetherian ring \(A\), one may find a complete local Noetherian ring \(B\), and a local homomorphism \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\), such that \(B\) is flat over \(A\) and that \(B/\mathfrak{m}B\) is a given extension of the residue field \(k=A/\mathfrak{m}\) of \(A\), one finds:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6

Under the conditions of 4, suppose moreover \(X'\) separated over \(S'\), and \(S\) locally Noetherian. For the descent datum on \(X'\) to be effective, it is necessary and sufficient that for every prescheme \(S_1\) over \(S\), spectrum of a complete local ring with algebraically closed residue field, the corresponding descent datum on \(X_1'=X'\times_S S_1\), relative to the morphism \(g_1\colon S_1'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_1\), be effective.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 7

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a finite and surjective morphism, and of finite presentation (this last hypothesis being a consequence of the others if \(S\) is locally Noetherian)1. Then \(g\) is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of preschemes étale, separated, of finite type over others.

It must be shown that if \(X'\) is étale, separated, of finite type over \(S'\), and equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\), then this datum is effective. Using 3, one reduces easily to the case where \(S\) is Noetherian. Thanks to 5, one may thus suppose that \(S\) is the spectrum of a local Noetherian ring, a fortiori that
\[ \dim S=n<+\infty. \]

One then argues by induction on \(\dim S=n\), the assertion being trivial for \(n<0\). Suppose then \(n\geqslant 0\) and the theorem proved for the dimensions \(n'<n\). By virtue of 6 one is reduced to the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of a complete local ring, hence \(S'\) is a finite union of spectra of complete local rings. One thus has
\[ X'=X_1'\sqcup X_2' \]
where \(X_1'\) is finite over \(S'\), and where \(X_2'\) has no point above any of the closed points of \(S'\). Consider the morphisms
\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{q_1,q_2\colon X''}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{X'}} \]
corresponding to the descent datum, compatible with \(\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{p_1,p_2\colon S''}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{S'}}\). One sees at once that
\[ X''=q_i^{-1}(X_1')\sqcup q_i^{-1}(X_2')\quad i=1,2 \]

is the analogous canonical decomposition of \(X''\) over \(S''\), which implies \(q_1^{-1}(X_1')= q_2^{-1}(X_1')\) and consequently \(X_1'\) and \(X_2'\) are endowed with induced descent data. Now let \(T\) be the open subset of \(S\) complementary to its closed point, so that \(T'=S'\times_S T\) is the part of \(S'\) complementary to the set of closed points, and \(X_2'\), which lies entirely above \(T'\), is endowed with a descent datum relative to the morphism \(T'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) induced by \(g\). Since the latter is finite surjective, and since \(\dim T<\dim S=n\), this descent datum is effective by the induction hypothesis. We thus see that it suffices to prove that the descent datum on \(X_1'\) is effective, so we may now assume \(X'\) étale and finite over \(S'\). (N.B. the argument by induction is unnecessary if one restricts to étale coverings in statement 7). Let then \(S_0\) be the spectrum of the residue field of \(A\), let \(S_0'=S'\times_S S_0\) and let us define likewise \(S_0''\), \(S_0'''\) from the fibred squares and cubes \(S''\) and \(S'''\) of \(S'\) over \(S\). By virtue of 8, the morphisms \(S_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), \(S_0'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\), etc. induce equivalences for the categories of étale coverings of \(S\) and \(S_0\) on the one hand, \(S'\) and \(S_0'\) on the other hand, etc. By the sorites of descent theory in categories [D], it follows that in order for \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) to be a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of étale coverings, it is necessary and sufficient that this be so for \(g_0\colon S_0'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_0\). But this is indeed the case, as a particular case of 1, for example. This completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8

The conclusion of 7 remains valid if one assumes only that \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is universally submersive, of finite type and quasi-finite, provided one assumes \(S\) locally Noetherian.

By virtue of 6, we may indeed assume that \(S\) is the spectrum of a complete Noetherian local ring. Then by virtue of 5, there exists a finite surjective morphism \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), and an \(S\)-morphism \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\). Since \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism of universal strict descent for the fibred category considered, by virtue of 7, and since \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism of universal descent for the said category, 8 follows from the general sorites [D].

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of finite type, surjective and universally open, with \(S\) locally Noetherian. Then \(g\) is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of preschemes étale, separated and of finite type over others.

Proceeding as in 7, we are reduced to the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of a complete Noetherian local ring \(A\). Let \(A_1\) be a finite algebra over \(A\), with spectrum \(S_1\), such that \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is finite and surjective, hence a morphism of universal effective descent for the fibred category considered, thanks to 7. It then follows from the general theorems [D] that \(g\) is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category considered if and only if the corresponding morphism \(g_1\colon S_1'=S'\times_S S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_1\) is. Since the latter satisfies the same hypotheses as \(g\), we are reduced to proving 9 for \(S_1\) instead of \(S\). Taking first for \(A_1\) the direct composite of the \(A/\mathfrak{p}_i\), for the minimal prime ideals \(\mathfrak{p}_i\) of \(A\), we are reduced to the case where \(A\) is integral. One then shows2 that there exists an integral subscheme \(S_1\) of \(S'\), quasi-finite over \(S\) and dominating \(S\), passing through a point of the fibre of \(S'\) at the closed point \(y\) of \(S\) (thanks to the fact that \(S'\) is universally open of finite type over \(S\) local Noetherian integral, and \(S_y'\neq\emptyset\)). Since \(A\) is complete, \(S_1\) is finite over \(S\), and since it dominates \(S\), the morphism \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is surjective. Replacing once more \(S\) by \(S_1\), we are reduced to the case where \(S'\) has a section over \(S\), where the statement is trivial.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 10

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a finite radicial surjective morphism, of finite presentation (this last condition being superfluous if \(S\) is locally Noetherian3). Then the inverse image functor induces an equivalence of the category of preschemes étale over \(S\) with the category of preschemes étale over \(S'\).

Since the diagonal morphisms of \(S'\) into \(S'\times_S S'\) and \(S'\times_S S'\times_S S'\) are surjective immersions, hence induce by virtue of 9 equivalences of the categories of preschemes étale over \(S'\times_S S'\) resp. \(S'\times_S S'\times_S S'\) with the category of preschemes étale over \(S'\), it follows from the sorites of descent [D] that every \(X'\) étale over \(S'\) is endowed with a descent datum, and with only one, relative to \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). Hence 3 implies that the inverse image functor by \(g\), from the category of preschemes étale over \(S\) into the category of preschemes étale over \(S'\), is fully faithful. It remains to show that it is essentially surjective, i.e. that every \(X'\) étale over \(S'\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of an \(X\) étale over \(S\). The question being obviously local on \(S\) and on \(X'\), we may assume \(S\), \(S'\), \(X'\) affine. But then \(X'\) is separated of finite type over \(S'\), and we may apply the effectivity criterion 7.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 11

Conclusion 9 remains valid on replacing the hypothesis on \(g\) by: \(g\) is faithfully flat, quasi-compact and radicial.
Same proof, invoking 1 instead of 7.

One will note that the proof of 7 is “elementary” in that it does not use the theorems of finiteness and of comparison for proper morphisms (EGA III 3, 4, 5). This is no longer the case for the following result:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 12

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper, surjective morphism, of finite presentation (this last hypothesis being a consequence of the first if \(S\) is locally Noetherian). Then \(g\) is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of étale coverings of preschemes.

By virtue of 3 and 2, we are reduced to proving that for every étale covering \(X'\) over \(S'\), equipped with a descent datum relative to \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), this descent datum is effective. Using 3, we are easily reduced to the case where \(S\) is Noetherian, and using 6, we may therefore suppose that \(S\) is the spectrum of a complete Noetherian local ring \(A\). Let us introduce \(S''\) and \(S'''\) as usual, let \(S_0\) be the spectrum of the residue field of \(A\), and let \(S_0'\), \(S_0''\), \(S_0'''\) be deduced from \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\) by the base change \(S_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), i.e. the fibres of \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\) at the closed point of \(S\). By 10, the morphisms \(S_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), \(S_0'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\), etc. induce equivalences of the category of étale coverings on the target scheme with the category of étale coverings on the source scheme. Consequently, \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a strict descent morphism for the fibred category of étale coverings of preschemes if and only if \(g_0\colon S_0'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_0\) is, which is indeed the case by virtue of 1. This completes the proof of 12. (In this argument, we needed of 10 only the fact that the functor considered in 10 is fully faithful, which does not use the existence theorem for coherent sheaves in algebraic geometry.)

footnotes.html: One can show that it in fact suffices that \(g\) be an integral morphism, by reducing to the case of the text by a process of passage to the limit in the style of EGA IV 8.

It even
suffices that \(g\) be integral, radicial surjective, as one sees by
an easy reduction to the case of the text, style EGA IV 8, cf. SGA 4
VIII 1.1.

pages.title: Translation in terms of the fundamental group
pages.html:
5 Translation in terms of the fundamental group

Let

\[ g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S \]

be a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of étale coverings of preschemes, for example a proper, surjective morphism of finite presentation (12), or a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism. Introducing as usual \(S''\), \(S'''\), and denoting by \(\mathscr{C}\), \(\mathscr{C}'\), \(\mathscr{C}''\), \(\mathscr{C}'''\) the category of étale coverings of \(S\), \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\) respectively, we thus have a \(2\)-exact diagram of categories

\[ \tag{*} \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathscr{C}}\ar[r]^-{~p^*}&{\mathscr{C}'}\ar@<2pt>[rr]^{p_1^*,p_2^*}\ar@<-2pt>[rr]&&{\mathscr{C}''}\ar@<4pt>[rrr]^{p_{21}^*,p_{32}^*,p_{31}^*}\ar[rrr]\ar@<-4pt>[rrr]&&&{\mathscr{C}'''}} \]

corresponding to the diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{S}&{S'}\ar[l]_-{~p}&&{S''}\ar@<2pt>[ll]\ar@<-2pt>[ll]_-{p_1,p_2}&&&{S'''.}\ar@<4pt>[lll]\ar[lll]\ar@<-4pt>[lll]_{p_{21},p_{32},p_{31}}} \]

Suppose the preschemes \(S\), \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\) are disjoint sums of connected preschemes, which will be the case in particular if they are locally connected preschemes, a fortiori if they are locally Noetherian (for example if \(S'\) is of finite type over \(S\) locally Noetherian). Then the categories \(\mathscr{C}\), \(\mathscr{C}'\) ... in (*) are multigaloisian categories V 9, each described by a collection of totally disconnected compact topological groups, namely the fundamental groups of the connected components of the preschemes \(S\), \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\). For simplicity we suppose \(S\) connected, and shall then give a procedure for computing its fundamental group, in terms of the fibred category formed with \(\mathscr{C}'\), \(\mathscr{C}''\), \(\mathscr{C}'''\), suitably made explicit by means of the fundamental groups expressing these categories. The reader will note that the sketched procedure is in fact valid within the general framework of multigaloisian categories (which need not arise from given preschemes \(S\), \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\)). It is moreover the analogue of the well-known procedure for computing the fundamental group of a topological space \(S\), a locally finite union of closed subspaces \(S_i\) (or an arbitrary union of open subspaces \(S_i\)), by means of the fundamental groups of the components of the \(S_i\) and of the components of the \(S_i\cap S_j\). Of course, the analogous situation within the framework of preschemes falls indeed within the general framework of descent, by introducing the prescheme \(S'\) sum of the \(S_i\) and the canonical morphism \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\).

Set

\[ E'=\pi_0(S'), \quad E''=\pi_0(S''), \quad E'''=\pi_0(S'''), \]

where \(\pi_0\) denotes the functor “set of connected components”. Since the fibre products of \(S'\) over \(S\) form a simplicial object of \(\mathbf{Sch}\), it is transformed by the functor \(\pi_0\) into a simplicial set of which \(E'\), \(E''\), \(E'''\) are the components of dimension \(0\), \(1\), \(2\). We shall have to use the simplicial maps

\[ q_i=\pi_0(p_i),\quad(i=1,2)\quad\text{and} \quad q_{ij}=\pi_0(p_{ij}),\quad (i,j)=(2,1),(3,2),(3,1), \]

displayed in the diagram

\[ \tag{1} {\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{E'}&&{E''}\ar@<2pt>[ll]\ar@<-2pt>[ll]_{q_1,q_2}&&&{E'''.}\ar@<4pt>[lll]\ar[lll]\ar@<-4pt>[lll]_{q_{21},q_{32},q_{31}}}} \]

The objects of \(E'\) will be denoted with a prime, like \(s'\), those of \(E''\) resp. \(E'''\) will be denoted with a \(''\) resp. a \('''\). The fact that \(S\) is connected translates into \(\pi_0(K)=0\), where \(K\) is the simplicial set defined by \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), or again by the fact that the equivalence relation in \(E'\) generated by the pair of maps \((q_1,q_2)\) is transitive.

We shall choose once and for all an element \(s_0'\) in \(E'\), and for every \(s'\) in \(E'\), an element \(\overline{s'}\in E''\) such that1

\[ q_1(\overline{s'})=s_0', \quad q_2(\overline{s'})=s', \]

thus displaying the connectedness of \(S\). For every \(s'\in E'\), let us choose a geometric point \(\underline{s}'\) in the connected component \(s'\) of \(S'\); this point will in fact intervene through the corresponding fibre-functor \(F_{s'}'\) on the multigaloisian category \(\mathscr{C}'\). The group of automorphisms of this functor, i.e. the fundamental group of \(S'\) at \(\underline{s}'\), will be denoted \(\pi_{s'}\). One likewise chooses \(\underline{s}''\) and \(\underline{s}'''\), hence functors \(F_{s''}''\) and \(F_{s'''}'''\), whence fundamental groups \(\pi_{s''}\) and \(\pi_{s'''}\). Thus

\[ \pi_{s'}=\pi_1(S',\underline{s}'), \quad \pi_{s''}=\pi_1(S'',\underline{s}''), \quad \pi_{s'''}=\pi_1(S''',\underline{s}'''). \]

For every \(s''\in E''\), \(p_1(\underline{s}'')\) lies in the same connected component as \(\underline{q_1(s'')}\), hence there exists an isomorphism of functors \(F''_{s''}\circ p_1^*\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} F'_{s'}\) (i.e. a “class of paths” from \(p_1(\underline{s}'')\) to \(\underline{q_1(s'')}\)). This remark is repeated for \(q_2\), and the \(q_{ij}\). Let us choose all these classes of paths:

\[ F''_{s''}\circ p_i^*\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} F'_{q_i(s'')},\quad F_{s'''}'''\circ p_{ij}^*\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} F'''_{q_{ij}(s''')}, \]

(for \(i=1,2\) and \((i,j)=(2,1),(3,2),(3,1)\)). There follow in particular homomorphisms of groups:

\[ \tag{2} {q_i^{s''}\colon \pi_{s''}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_{q_i(s'')},\quad q_{ij}^{s'''}\colon \pi_{s'''}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_{q_{ij}(s''')},} \]

(same values of \(i\) and \((i,j)\)). Finally, let us recall that in the cleaved-category structure with fibres \(\mathscr{C}'\), \(\mathscr{C''}\), \(\mathscr{C'''}\) there also appear isomorphisms of functors:

\[ p_{21}^*p_1^*\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_{31}^*p_1^*,\quad p_{21}^*p_2^*\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_{32}^*p_1^*,\quad p_{31}^*p_2^*\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_{32}^*p_2^*, \]

deduced from isomorphisms of the two sides respectively with the \(u_i^*\) (\(i=1,2,3\)), where the \(u_i\) are the three projections of \(S'''\) into \(S'\). When one makes these data explicit, one finds for every \(s'''\) a well-determined element

\[ \tag{3} {a_i^{s'''}\in\pi_{v_i(s''')},} \]

(where the \(v_i\), \(i=1,2,3\) are the three maps \(E'''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E'\) defined by \(v_i=\pi_0(u_i)\)), moreover subject to the conditions:

\[ q_1^{s_1''}q_{21}^{s'''}=\mathrm{int}(a_1^{s'''}) q_1^{s_2''}q_{31}^{s'''}\qquad (s_1''=q_{21}(s'''),\ s_2''=q_{31}(s''')), \]

and the two analogous conditions, involving the \(a_2\) and \(a_3\). The reader will note moreover that the data (1), (2), (3) allow one to reconstitute, up to an equivalence of fibred categories, the fibred category under consideration with fibres \(\mathscr{C}'\), \(\mathscr{C}''\), \(\mathscr{C}'''\). They must therefore in principle allow one to reconstitute \(\mathscr{C}\) up to equivalence, hence its fundamental group up to isomorphism. We shall in fact determine the fundamental group at the geometric point \(p(\underline{s}_0')\) of \(S\), i.e. the group of automorphisms of \(F'_{s_0'}\circ p^*\).

One notes that the datum of an object \(X'\) of \(\mathscr{C}'\) is essentially equivalent to the datum of finite sets \(X'_{s'}\) (\(s'\in E'\)) on which the \(\pi_{s'}\) operate continuously. A descent datum on such an object then amounts to the datum, for every \(s''\in E''\), of a bijection:

\[ \varphi_{s''}\colon X'_{q_1(s'')}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X'_{q_2(s'')} \]

compatible with the operations of \(\pi_{s''}\), operating on both sides via the homomorphisms \(q_i^{s''}\colon \pi_{s''}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_{q_i(s'')}\). Taking first the \(s''\) of the form \(\overline{s'}\), one sees that such a datum defines bijections

\[ \psi_{s'}\colon X'_{s_0'}=F_0'(X')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{s'}' \]

which allows one to identify the \(X'_{s'}\) with the same set \(F_0'(X')=X'_{s_0'}\), on which all the groups \(\pi_{s'}\) will thenceforth operate. This being so, the bijections \(\varphi_{s''}\) will correspond to bijections

\[ g_{s''}\colon F_0'(X')\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} F_0'(X'), \]

subject on the one hand to the commutation relations with \(\pi_{s''}\):

\[ \tag*{a)} {g_{s''}q_1^{s''}(g'')=q_2^{s''}(g'')g_{s''}\qquad (s''\in E'',\ g''\in\pi_{s''}),} \]

on the other hand to the relations

\[ \tag*{b)} {g_{\overline{s'}}=g_{\overline{s_0'}}\qquad (s'\in E'),} \]

expressing the way in which we had identified the \(X_{s'}\) with one another. When one makes explicit the condition for such a gluing datum to be in fact a descent datum, one finds the relations:

\[ \tag*{c)} {a_3^{s'''}g_{q_{31}(s''')}a_1^{s'''}= g_{q_{32}(s''')}a_2^{s'''}g_{q_{21}(s''')}\qquad (s'''\in E''').} \]

This gives us an equivalence between the category of objects of \(\mathscr{C}'\) equipped with a descent datum, and the category of finite sets on which the groups \(\pi_{s'}\) operate continuously, further equipped with bijections \(g_{s''}\), satisfying the relations a), b), c). Let then \(G\) be the group generated by the groups \(\pi_{s'}\) and the new generators \(g_{s''}\), subject to the relations a), b), c), and let \(\pi\) be the inverse limit group of the quotients of \(G\) by the subgroups of finite index whose inverse images in the groups \(\pi_{s'}\) are open subgroups. One also says that \(\pi\) is the group of Galois type generated by the \(\pi_{s'}\) and the \(g_{s''}\), subject to the relations a), b), c). One observes at once that the category under consideration is also equivalent to the category of finite sets on which the topological group \(\pi\) operates continuously. This establishes the following statement:

Theorem 1

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of preschemes which is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of étale coverings of preschemes (cf. 9 and 12). Suppose \(S\) connected, and \(S'\), its fibred square \(S''\) and its fibred cube \(S'''\), sums of connected preschemes (which is the case for example if \(S'\) is of finite type over \(S\) locally Noetherian and connected). Let us choose as above: a geometric point in each connected component of \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\), certain classes of paths, an \(s_0'\in E'\), and for every \(s'\in E'\) an \(s''\in E''\) whose two images in \(E'\) are \(s_0'\) and \(s'\). (\(E'\), \(E''\), \(E'''\) denote respectively the set of connected components of \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\)). Then the fundamental group of \(S\) at the geometric point image of \(s_0'\) is canonically isomorphic to the group of Galois type generated by the \(\pi_{s'}=\pi_1(S',\underline{s}')\) (\(s'\in E'\)) and by generators \(g_{s''}\) (\(s''\in E''\)), subject to the relations a), b), c) above involving the elements of the groups \(\pi_{s''}=\pi_1(S'',\underline{s}'')\), and the elements \(a_i^{s'''}\) (\(i=1,2,3\), \(s'''\in E'''\)) introduced above.

Corollary 2

Suppose that \(S'\) and \(S''\) have only a finite number of connected components, and that the fundamental groups of the connected components of \(S'\) are topologically finitely generated. Then the fundamental group of \(S\) is topologically finitely generated.

Thus, we shall prove later that the fundamental group of a projective normal scheme over an algebraically closed field is topologically finitely generated. Using Chow's lemma and the normalization of algebraic schemes, it will follow that the same result is true for every scheme proper over an algebraically closed field.

Corollary 3

Suppose that \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\) have only a finite number of connected components, that the fundamental groups of the connected components of \(S'\) are topologically finitely presented, and the fundamental groups of the connected components of \(S''\) topologically finitely generated. Then the fundamental group of \(S\) is topologically finitely presented.

One will note that one can express 9 (restricted to étale coverings) by saying that a finite radicial surjective morphism of Noetherian preschemes induces an isomorphism of the fundamental groups; figuratively speaking, one may therefore say that the fundamental group is a topological invariant for preschemes. One can make explicit more generally, by means of 1, the effect on the fundamental group of operations on preschemes, such as the “pinching” of the prescheme along a finite set of points, having a simple topological meaning. One finds for example:

Corollary 4

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a finite morphism of finite presentation, \(T\) a discrete part of \(S\). For every \(s\in S\), let \(n(s)\) be the “geometric number of points” in the fibre \(g^{-1}(s)\) (which is also made explicit as the separable degree of \(g^{-1}(s)\) over \(\mathit{k}(s)\), the sum of the separable degrees of its residual extensions). One supposes that for \(s\in S-T\), one has \(n(s)=1\). For every \(s\in T\), let \(K_s\) be an algebraically closed extension of \(\mathit{k}(s)\), \(I_s\) the set of geometric points of \(S'\) with values in \(K_s\) (it is a set with \(n(s)\) elements), \(I_s'\) the complement of a chosen point of \(I_s\), and finally \(I'\) the union set of the \(I_s'\). One supposes \(S'\) connected. Then the fundamental group of \(S\) is isomorphic to the group of Galois type generated by the fundamental group of \(S'\), and by generators \(g_i\) (\(i\in I'\)), subject to no supplementary condition.

The detail of the proof is left to the reader; the statement obtained is only the translation, into the language of the theory of groups, of the fact that one has an equivalence of the category \(C\) of étale coverings of \(S\), and of the category of étale coverings \(X'\) of \(S'\), equipped for every \(s\in T\) with a transitive system of bijections between the \(n(s)\) fibres of \(X'\) at the points of \(g^{-1}(s)\) with values in \(K_s\). (In this intrinsic form, of course, it is no longer necessary to suppose \(S'\) connected).

Example 5

One proves easily that the rational curve \(\mathbf{P}^1_k\) over an algebraically closed field \(k\) is simply connected2. Therefore the fundamental group of a complete rational curve having exactly one double point, with \(n\) analytic branches, is the group of Galois type freely generated by \(n-1\) generators. For example, in the case of an ordinary double point, one finds the fundamental group \(\widehat{\mathbf{Z}}\), as announced in (I 11 a)). On the contrary, the existence of a cusp (which is a point “geometrically unibranch”) has no influence on the fundamental group.

Corollary 6

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of preschemes universally submersive, with geometrically connected fibres, \(S\) being connected. Then \(S'\) is connected, and choosing a geometric point \(s'\) in \(S'\) and denoting by \(s\) its image in \(S\), the homomorphism

\[ \pi_1(S',s')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(S,s) \]

is surjective. If \(g\) is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of étale coverings of preschemes (cf. 12), introducing the geometric point \(s^{\prime\prime}=\operatorname{diag}(s')\) of \(S^{\prime\prime}=S'\times_S S'\), and the two homomorphisms

\[ p_{1*},p_{2*}\colon \pi_1(S^{\prime\prime},s^{\prime\prime}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(S',s') \]

induced by the two projections, \(\pi_1(S, s)\) is isomorphic to the cokernel of this pair of morphisms in the category of groups of Galois type, i.e. to the quotient of \(\pi_1(S',s')\) by the closed invariant subgroup generated by the elements of the form \(p_{1*}(g^{\prime\prime})p_{2*}(g^{\prime\prime})^{-1}\), with \(g^{\prime\prime}\in \pi_1(S^{\prime\prime},s^{\prime\prime})\).

One knows indeed by 4 that the functor \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X\times_SS'\) from the étale coverings over \(S\) to the étale coverings over \(S'\) is fully faithful, which amounts to the fact that the homomorphism on the fundamental groups is an epimorphism (V 9). The last assertion is an immediate consequence of the description 1.

Remark 7

It is not known at present whether the fundamental group of a proper scheme over an algebraically closed field \(k\) is topologically of finite presentation3. Using 3, a well-known technique of hyperplane sections, and the desingularization of normal surfaces, one is reduced to the case of a smooth surface over \(k\). This at least allows one to show, by transcendental means, that the answer is affirmative in characteristic \(0\) (and this without being obliged to admit the triangulability of singular algebraic varieties). In characteristic \(p>0\), the principal difficulty seems to lie in the case of curves, of which one knows only that the fundamental group is a quotient of the one which presents itself in the classical case (cf. following exposé), the kernel by which one divides being however very poorly known.

Remark 8

One could make explicit other particular cases than 4 and 6 where 1 takes a particularly simple form. An interesting case is that where \(S\) is the quotient of \(S'\) by a finite group of automorphisms \(\Gamma\). Then the category of étale coverings of \(S'\) is equivalent to the category of étale coverings \(X'\) of \(S'\), where the group \(\Gamma\) operates compatibly with its operations on \(S'\), in such a way that for every \(s'\in S'\) and every \(g\in \Gamma_{s'}\) (where \(\Gamma_{s'}\) denotes the inertia group of \(s'\) in \(\Gamma\)), \(g\) operates trivially on the fibre \(X_{s'}^\prime\). If \(S'\) is connected this statement is interpreted in the following way. Let \(C'_0\) be the category of étale coverings of \(S'\) where \(\Gamma\) operates compatibly with its operations on \(S'\) (but without necessarily satisfying the condition above on the inertia groups of the points of \(S'\)). One sees easily that it is a Galois category (V 5), and that for every geometric point \(a'\) of \(S'\), the fibre functor \(X'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X_{a'}'\) on \(C'_0\) is a fundamental functor. Let \(\pi_1(S',\Gamma;a')=G\) be the group of automorphisms of this functor, equipped with its usual topology. One then has a canonical exact sequence

\[ e\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(S',a')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e \]

(a particular case of (V 13), where one takes for \(S\) the trivial covering \(S'\times \Gamma\) of \(S'\) defined by \(\Gamma\), where one lets \(\Gamma\) operate in the obvious way). Moreover for every geometric point \(b'\) of \(S'\), one has an isomorphism \(\pi_1(S', \Gamma ; b' )\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G = \pi_1(S', \Gamma; a')\) defined up to an inner automorphism coming from \(\pi_1(S',a')\), and since \(\Gamma_{b'}\) maps in the obvious way into the first member, one obtains a homomorphism

\[ u_{b'}\colon \Gamma_{b'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G , \]

defined up to an inner automorphism (coming from \(\pi_1(S',a')\)), whose composite with the canonical homomorphism \(G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma\) is moreover the canonical immersion \(\Gamma_{b'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma\). This being so, the fundamental group \(\pi_1(S,a)\) is canonically isomorphic to the quotient group of \(G = \pi_1(S', \Gamma; a')\) by the closed invariant subgroup generated by the images of the homomorphisms \(\Gamma_{b'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\). In particular, the image of \(\pi_1(S',a')\) in \(\pi_1(S,a)\) is an invariant subgroup, and the corresponding quotient is isomorphic to a quotient of \(\Gamma\). One can moreover reduce the number of “relations” introduced by introducing, for every \(g\in \Gamma\), \( g\neq e\), the sub-prescheme \(S'_g\) of the coincidences of the automorphisms \(\mathrm{id}_S\) and \(g\) of \(S\), by choosing a geometric point \(b'_{g,i}\) in each connected component of \(S'_g\), then one of the corresponding homomorphisms \(\pi_1(S',\Gamma; b'_{g,i})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\), whence liftings \(\overline{g}_i\) of \(g\) in \(G\). It then suffices to take the quotient of \(G\) by the closed invariant subgroup of \(G\) generated by the \(\overline{g}_i\).

When \(a'\) is invariant under \(\Gamma\), one sees easily that \(\Gamma\) operates naturally on \(\pi_1(S',a')\), and \(G\) is identified with the corresponding semi-direct product. Identifying then \(\Gamma\) with a subgroup of \(G\), one sees that in the relations introduced above, taking \(b'=a'\), one finds “ \(g = e\) ” for \(g\in I\). Hence if \(S'\) has a geometric point \(a'\) fixed by \(I\) (i.e. a point \(s'\) whose inertia group is \(\Gamma\)), then \(\pi_1(S,a)\) is a quotient group of the quotient group of Galois type of \(\pi_1(S',a')\) obtained by “ making trivial ” the operations of \(\Gamma\) on \(\pi_1(S',a')\); and it is even isomorphic to this last group if one supposes that for every \(g\in G\), the inertia set \(S_g'\) is connected, hence passes through the locality of \(a'\). This last assertion is indeed contained in the second description given above for the relations to be introduced in \(G\).

This last result applies in particular if one takes for \(S'\) the cartesian power \(X^n\) of a connected prescheme over an algebraically closed field, for \(\Gamma\) the symmetric group \(\Gamma={\mathfrak{S}}_n\), operating in the usual way, whence for \(S\) the symmetric power of \(S\). Taking then for \(a'\) a geometric point localized at the diagonal, one is under the conditions above, the inertia sets \(S_g'\) indeed all containing the diagonal. Using the fact, proved in the following exposé, that if \(X\) is proper connected over \(k\), the fundamental group of \(X^n\) is identified with \(\pi_1(X)^n\), one finds the following amusing result: If \(X\) is proper connected over \(k\) algebraically closed, the fundamental group of its symmetric power , \(n \geqslant 2\), is isomorphic to the fundamental group of \(X\) made abelian. (I do not know whether the analogous fact in algebraic Topology is known; it should be possible to establish it by the same method of descent). Taking for example for \(X\) a rational curve \(X=\mathbf{P}^1_k\), one finds a proof of the fact that \(\mathbf{P}^r_k\) is simply connected, using the fact that \(\mathbf{P}^1_k\) is so. Let us now take for \(X\) a simple curve over \(k\), and \(n\geqslant 2g-1\), so that \(\operatorname{Symm}^n(X)\) is fibred over the jacobian \(J\), with fibres projective spaces, hence (as one will see with the aid of the results of the two following exposés) has the same fundamental group as \(J\). One then recovers without dévissage the well-known fact that the fundamental group of the jacobian of \(X\) is isomorphic to the fundamental group of \(X\) made abelian.

blocks.html: 5 Translation in terms of the fundamental group
Let
\[ g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S \]

be a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of étale coverings of preschemes, for example a proper, surjective morphism of finite presentation (12), or a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism. Introducing as usual \(S''\), \(S'''\), and denoting by \(\mathscr{C}\), \(\mathscr{C}'\), \(\mathscr{C}''\), \(\mathscr{C}'''\) the category of étale coverings of \(S\), \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\) respectively, we thus have a \(2\)-exact diagram of categories

\[ \tag{*} \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathscr{C}}\ar[r]^-{~p^*}&{\mathscr{C}'}\ar@<2pt>[rr]^{p_1^*,p_2^*}\ar@<-2pt>[rr]&&{\mathscr{C}''}\ar@<4pt>[rrr]^{p_{21}^*,p_{32}^*,p_{31}^*}\ar[rrr]\ar@<-4pt>[rrr]&&&{\mathscr{C}'''}} \]
corresponding to the diagram
\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{S}&{S'}\ar[l]_-{~p}&&{S''}\ar@<2pt>[ll]\ar@<-2pt>[ll]_-{p_1,p_2}&&&{S'''.}\ar@<4pt>[lll]\ar[lll]\ar@<-4pt>[lll]_{p_{21},p_{32},p_{31}}} \]

Suppose the preschemes \(S\), \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\) are disjoint sums of connected preschemes, which will be the case in particular if they are locally connected preschemes, a fortiori if they are locally Noetherian (for example if \(S'\) is of finite type over \(S\) locally Noetherian).
