Then the categories \(\mathscr{C}\), \(\mathscr{C}'\) ... in (*) are multigaloisian categories V 9, each described by a collection of totally disconnected compact topological groups, namely the fundamental groups of the connected components of the preschemes \(S\), \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\). For simplicity we suppose \(S\) connected, and shall then give a procedure for computing its fundamental group, in terms of the fibred category formed with \(\mathscr{C}'\), \(\mathscr{C}''\), \(\mathscr{C}'''\), suitably made explicit by means of the fundamental groups expressing these categories. The reader will note that the sketched procedure is in fact valid within the general framework of multigaloisian categories (which need not arise from given preschemes \(S\), \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\)). It is moreover the analogue of the well-known procedure for computing the fundamental group of a topological space \(S\), a locally finite union of closed subspaces \(S_i\) (or an arbitrary union of open subspaces \(S_i\)), by means of the fundamental groups of the components of the \(S_i\) and of the components of the \(S_i\cap S_j\). Of course, the analogous situation within the framework of preschemes falls indeed within the general framework of descent, by introducing the prescheme \(S'\) sum of the \(S_i\) and the canonical morphism \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\).
Set
\[ E'=\pi_0(S'), \quad E''=\pi_0(S''), \quad E'''=\pi_0(S'''), \]

where \(\pi_0\) denotes the functor “set of connected components”. Since the fibre products of \(S'\) over \(S\) form a simplicial object of \(\mathbf{Sch}\), it is transformed by the functor \(\pi_0\) into a simplicial set of which \(E'\), \(E''\), \(E'''\) are the components of dimension \(0\), \(1\), \(2\). We shall have to use the simplicial maps
\[ q_i=\pi_0(p_i),\quad(i=1,2)\quad\text{and} \quad q_{ij}=\pi_0(p_{ij}),\quad (i,j)=(2,1),(3,2),(3,1), \]
displayed in the diagram
\[ \tag{1} {\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{E'}&&{E''}\ar@<2pt>[ll]\ar@<-2pt>[ll]_{q_1,q_2}&&&{E'''.}\ar@<4pt>[lll]\ar[lll]\ar@<-4pt>[lll]_{q_{21},q_{32},q_{31}}}} \]

The objects of \(E'\) will be denoted with a prime, like \(s'\), those of \(E''\) resp. \(E'''\) will be denoted with a \(''\) resp. a \('''\). The fact that \(S\) is connected translates into \(\pi_0(K)=0\), where \(K\) is the simplicial set defined by \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), or again by the fact that the equivalence relation in \(E'\) generated by the pair of maps \((q_1,q_2)\) is transitive.
We shall choose once and for all an element \(s_0'\) in \(E'\), and for every \(s'\) in \(E'\), an element \(\overline{s'}\in E''\) such that1
\[ q_1(\overline{s'})=s_0', \quad q_2(\overline{s'})=s', \]

thus displaying the connectedness of \(S\). For every \(s'\in E'\), let us choose a geometric point \(\underline{s}'\) in the connected component \(s'\) of \(S'\); this point will in fact intervene through the corresponding fibre-functor \(F_{s'}'\) on the multigaloisian category \(\mathscr{C}'\). The group of automorphisms of this functor, i.e. the fundamental group of \(S'\) at \(\underline{s}'\), will be denoted \(\pi_{s'}\). One likewise chooses \(\underline{s}''\) and \(\underline{s}'''\), hence functors \(F_{s''}''\) and \(F_{s'''}'''\), whence fundamental groups \(\pi_{s''}\) and \(\pi_{s'''}\). Thus
\[ \pi_{s'}=\pi_1(S',\underline{s}'), \quad \pi_{s''}=\pi_1(S'',\underline{s}''), \quad \pi_{s'''}=\pi_1(S''',\underline{s}'''). \]

For every \(s''\in E''\), \(p_1(\underline{s}'')\) lies in the same connected component as \(\underline{q_1(s'')}\), hence there exists an isomorphism of functors \(F''_{s''}\circ p_1^*\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} F'_{s'}\) (i.e. a “class of paths” from \(p_1(\underline{s}'')\) to \(\underline{q_1(s'')}\)). This remark is repeated for \(q_2\), and the \(q_{ij}\). Let us choose all these classes of paths:
\[ F''_{s''}\circ p_i^*\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} F'_{q_i(s'')},\quad F_{s'''}'''\circ p_{ij}^*\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} F'''_{q_{ij}(s''')}, \]
(for \(i=1,2\) and \((i,j)=(2,1),(3,2),(3,1)\)). There follow in particular homomorphisms of groups:

\[ \tag{2} {q_i^{s''}\colon \pi_{s''}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_{q_i(s'')},\quad q_{ij}^{s'''}\colon \pi_{s'''}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_{q_{ij}(s''')},} \]

(same values of \(i\) and \((i,j)\)). Finally, let us recall that in the cleaved-category structure with fibres \(\mathscr{C}'\), \(\mathscr{C''}\), \(\mathscr{C'''}\) there also appear isomorphisms of functors:
\[ p_{21}^*p_1^*\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_{31}^*p_1^*,\quad p_{21}^*p_2^*\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_{32}^*p_1^*,\quad p_{31}^*p_2^*\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_{32}^*p_2^*, \]

deduced from isomorphisms of the two sides respectively with the \(u_i^*\) (\(i=1,2,3\)), where the \(u_i\) are the three projections of \(S'''\) into \(S'\). When one makes these data explicit, one finds for every \(s'''\) a well-determined element
\[ \tag{3} {a_i^{s'''}\in\pi_{v_i(s''')},} \]
(where the \(v_i\), \(i=1,2,3\) are the three maps \(E'''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E'\) defined by \(v_i=\pi_0(u_i)\)), moreover subject to the conditions:

and the two analogous conditions, involving the \(a_2\) and \(a_3\). The reader will note moreover that the data (1), (2), (3) allow one to reconstitute, up to an equivalence of fibred categories, the fibred category under consideration with fibres \(\mathscr{C}'\), \(\mathscr{C}''\), \(\mathscr{C}'''\). They must therefore in principle allow one to reconstitute \(\mathscr{C}\) up to equivalence, hence its fundamental group up to isomorphism. We shall in fact determine the fundamental group at the geometric point \(p(\underline{s}_0')\) of \(S\), i.e. the group of automorphisms of \(F'_{s_0'}\circ p^*\).

One notes that the datum of an object \(X'\) of \(\mathscr{C}'\) is essentially equivalent to the datum of finite sets \(X'_{s'}\) (\(s'\in E'\)) on which the \(\pi_{s'}\) operate continuously. A descent datum on such an object then amounts to the datum, for every \(s''\in E''\), of a bijection:
\[ \varphi_{s''}\colon X'_{q_1(s'')}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X'_{q_2(s'')} \]

compatible with the operations of \(\pi_{s''}\), operating on both sides via the homomorphisms \(q_i^{s''}\colon \pi_{s''}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_{q_i(s'')}\). Taking first the \(s''\) of the form \(\overline{s'}\), one sees that such a datum defines bijections
\[ \psi_{s'}\colon X'_{s_0'}=F_0'(X')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{s'}' \]

which allows one to identify the \(X'_{s'}\) with the same set \(F_0'(X')=X'_{s_0'}\), on which all the groups \(\pi_{s'}\) will thenceforth operate. This being so, the bijections \(\varphi_{s''}\) will correspond to bijections
\[ g_{s''}\colon F_0'(X')\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} F_0'(X'), \]
subject on the one hand to the commutation relations with \(\pi_{s''}\):

blocks.label: a)
blocks.html: \[ \tag*{a)} {g_{s''}q_1^{s''}(g'')=q_2^{s''}(g'')g_{s''}\qquad (s''\in E'',\ g''\in\pi_{s''}),} \]
on the other hand to the relations

blocks.label: b)
blocks.html: \[ \tag*{b)} {g_{\overline{s'}}=g_{\overline{s_0'}}\qquad (s'\in E'),} \]
expressing the way in which we had identified the \(X_{s'}\) with one another. When one makes explicit the condition for such a gluing datum to be in fact a descent datum, one finds the relations:

This gives us an equivalence between the category of objects of \(\mathscr{C}'\) equipped with a descent datum, and the category of finite sets on which the groups \(\pi_{s'}\) operate continuously, further equipped with bijections \(g_{s''}\), satisfying the relations a), b), c). Let then \(G\) be the group generated by the groups \(\pi_{s'}\) and the new generators \(g_{s''}\), subject to the relations a), b), c), and let \(\pi\) be the inverse limit group of the quotients of \(G\) by the subgroups of finite index whose inverse images in the groups \(\pi_{s'}\) are open subgroups. One also says that \(\pi\) is the group of Galois type generated by the \(\pi_{s'}\) and the \(g_{s''}\), subject to the relations a), b), c). One observes at once that the category under consideration is also equivalent to the category of finite sets on which the topological group \(\pi\) operates continuously. This establishes the following statement:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of preschemes which is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of étale coverings of preschemes (cf. 9 and 12). Suppose \(S\) connected, and \(S'\), its fibred square \(S''\) and its fibred cube \(S'''\), sums of connected preschemes (which is the case for example if \(S'\) is of finite type over \(S\) locally Noetherian and connected). Let us choose as above: a geometric point in each connected component of \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\), certain classes of paths, an \(s_0'\in E'\), and for every \(s'\in E'\) an \(s''\in E''\) whose two images in \(E'\) are \(s_0'\) and \(s'\). (\(E'\), \(E''\), \(E'''\) denote respectively the set of connected components of \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\)). Then the fundamental group of \(S\) at the geometric point image of \(s_0'\) is canonically isomorphic to the group of Galois type generated by the \(\pi_{s'}=\pi_1(S',\underline{s}')\) (\(s'\in E'\)) and by generators \(g_{s''}\) (\(s''\in E''\)), subject to the relations a), b), c) above involving the elements of the groups \(\pi_{s''}=\pi_1(S'',\underline{s}'')\), and the elements \(a_i^{s'''}\) (\(i=1,2,3\), \(s'''\in E'''\)) introduced above.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

Suppose that \(S'\) and \(S''\) have only a finite number of connected components, and that the fundamental groups of the connected components of \(S'\) are topologically finitely generated. Then the fundamental group of \(S\) is topologically finitely generated.

Thus, we shall prove later that the fundamental group of a projective normal scheme over an algebraically closed field is topologically finitely generated. Using Chow's lemma and the normalization of algebraic schemes, it will follow that the same result is true for every scheme proper over an algebraically closed field.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

Suppose that \(S'\), \(S''\), \(S'''\) have only a finite number of connected components, that the fundamental groups of the connected components of \(S'\) are topologically finitely presented, and the fundamental groups of the connected components of \(S''\) topologically finitely generated. Then the fundamental group of \(S\) is topologically finitely presented.

One will note that one can express 9 (restricted to étale coverings) by saying that a finite radicial surjective morphism of Noetherian preschemes induces an isomorphism of the fundamental groups; figuratively speaking, one may therefore say that the fundamental group is a topological invariant for preschemes. One can make explicit more generally, by means of 1, the effect on the fundamental group of operations on preschemes, such as the “pinching” of the prescheme along a finite set of points, having a simple topological meaning. One finds for example:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a finite morphism of finite presentation, \(T\) a discrete part of \(S\). For every \(s\in S\), let \(n(s)\) be the “geometric number of points” in the fibre \(g^{-1}(s)\) (which is also made explicit as the separable degree of \(g^{-1}(s)\) over \(\mathit{k}(s)\), the sum of the separable degrees of its residual extensions). One supposes that for \(s\in S-T\), one has \(n(s)=1\). For every \(s\in T\), let \(K_s\) be an algebraically closed extension of \(\mathit{k}(s)\), \(I_s\) the set of geometric points of \(S'\) with values in \(K_s\) (it is a set with \(n(s)\) elements), \(I_s'\) the complement of a chosen point of \(I_s\), and finally \(I'\) the union set of the \(I_s'\). One supposes \(S'\) connected. Then the fundamental group of \(S\) is isomorphic to the group of Galois type generated by the fundamental group of \(S'\), and by generators \(g_i\) (\(i\in I'\)), subject to no supplementary condition.

The detail of the proof is left to the reader; the statement obtained is only the translation, into the language of the theory of groups, of the fact that one has an equivalence of the category \(C\) of étale coverings of \(S\), and of the category of étale coverings \(X'\) of \(S'\), equipped for every \(s\in T\) with a transitive system of bijections between the \(n(s)\) fibres of \(X'\) at the points of \(g^{-1}(s)\) with values in \(K_s\). (In this intrinsic form, of course, it is no longer necessary to suppose \(S'\) connected).

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 5

One proves easily that the rational curve \(\mathbf{P}^1_k\) over an algebraically closed field \(k\) is simply connected2. Therefore the fundamental group of a complete rational curve having exactly one double point, with \(n\) analytic branches, is the group of Galois type freely generated by \(n-1\) generators. For example, in the case of an ordinary double point, one finds the fundamental group \(\widehat{\mathbf{Z}}\), as announced in (I 11 a)). On the contrary, the existence of a cusp (which is a point “geometrically unibranch”) has no influence on the fundamental group.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6

Let \(g\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of preschemes universally submersive, with geometrically connected fibres, \(S\) being connected. Then \(S'\) is connected, and choosing a geometric point \(s'\) in \(S'\) and denoting by \(s\) its image in \(S\), the homomorphism

\[ \pi_1(S',s')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(S,s) \]

is surjective. If \(g\) is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of étale coverings of preschemes (cf. 12), introducing the geometric point \(s^{\prime\prime}=\operatorname{diag}(s')\) of \(S^{\prime\prime}=S'\times_S S'\), and the two homomorphisms

\[ p_{1*},p_{2*}\colon \pi_1(S^{\prime\prime},s^{\prime\prime}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(S',s') \]

induced by the two projections, \(\pi_1(S, s)\) is isomorphic to the cokernel of this pair of morphisms in the category of groups of Galois type, i.e. to the quotient of \(\pi_1(S',s')\) by the closed invariant subgroup generated by the elements of the form \(p_{1*}(g^{\prime\prime})p_{2*}(g^{\prime\prime})^{-1}\), with \(g^{\prime\prime}\in \pi_1(S^{\prime\prime},s^{\prime\prime})\).

One knows indeed by 4 that the functor \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X\times_SS'\) from the étale coverings over \(S\) to the étale coverings over \(S'\) is fully faithful, which amounts to the fact that the homomorphism on the fundamental groups is an epimorphism (V 9). The last assertion is an immediate consequence of the description 1.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 7

It is not known at present whether the fundamental group of a proper scheme over an algebraically closed field \(k\) is topologically of finite presentation3. Using 3, a well-known technique of hyperplane sections, and the desingularization of normal surfaces, one is reduced to the case of a smooth surface over \(k\). This at least allows one to show, by transcendental means, that the answer is affirmative in characteristic \(0\) (and this without being obliged to admit the triangulability of singular algebraic varieties). In characteristic \(p>0\), the principal difficulty seems to lie in the case of curves, of which one knows only that the fundamental group is a quotient of the one which presents itself in the classical case (cf. following exposé), the kernel by which one divides being however very poorly known.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 8

One could make explicit other particular cases than 4 and 6 where 1 takes a particularly simple form. An interesting case is that where \(S\) is the quotient of \(S'\) by a finite group of automorphisms \(\Gamma\). Then the category of étale coverings of \(S'\) is equivalent to the category of étale coverings \(X'\) of \(S'\), where the group \(\Gamma\) operates compatibly with its operations on \(S'\), in such a way that for every \(s'\in S'\) and every \(g\in \Gamma_{s'}\) (where \(\Gamma_{s'}\) denotes the inertia group of \(s'\) in \(\Gamma\)), \(g\) operates trivially on the fibre \(X_{s'}^\prime\). If \(S'\) is connected this statement is interpreted in the following way. Let \(C'_0\) be the category of étale coverings of \(S'\) where \(\Gamma\) operates compatibly with its operations on \(S'\) (but without necessarily satisfying the condition above on the inertia groups of the points of \(S'\)). One sees easily that it is a Galois category (V 5), and that for every geometric point \(a'\) of \(S'\), the fibre functor \(X'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X_{a'}'\) on \(C'_0\) is a fundamental functor. Let \(\pi_1(S',\Gamma;a')=G\) be the group of automorphisms of this functor, equipped with its usual topology. One then has a canonical exact sequence

\[ e\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(S',a')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e \]

(a particular case of (V 13), where one takes for \(S\) the trivial covering \(S'\times \Gamma\) of \(S'\) defined by \(\Gamma\), where one lets \(\Gamma\) operate in the obvious way). Moreover for every geometric point \(b'\) of \(S'\), one has an isomorphism \(\pi_1(S', \Gamma ; b' )\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G = \pi_1(S', \Gamma; a')\) defined up to an inner automorphism coming from \(\pi_1(S',a')\), and since \(\Gamma_{b'}\) maps in the obvious way into the first member, one obtains a homomorphism

\[ u_{b'}\colon \Gamma_{b'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G , \]

defined up to an inner automorphism (coming from \(\pi_1(S',a')\)), whose composite with the canonical homomorphism \(G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma\) is moreover the canonical immersion \(\Gamma_{b'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma\). This being so, the fundamental group \(\pi_1(S,a)\) is canonically isomorphic to the quotient group of \(G = \pi_1(S', \Gamma; a')\) by the closed invariant subgroup generated by the images of the homomorphisms \(\Gamma_{b'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\). In particular, the image of \(\pi_1(S',a')\) in \(\pi_1(S,a)\) is an invariant subgroup, and the corresponding quotient is isomorphic to a quotient of \(\Gamma\). One can moreover reduce the number of “relations” introduced by introducing, for every \(g\in \Gamma\), \( g\neq e\), the sub-prescheme \(S'_g\) of the coincidences of the automorphisms \(\mathrm{id}_S\) and \(g\) of \(S\), by choosing a geometric point \(b'_{g,i}\) in each connected component of \(S'_g\), then one of the corresponding homomorphisms \(\pi_1(S',\Gamma; b'_{g,i})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\), whence liftings \(\overline{g}_i\) of \(g\) in \(G\). It then suffices to take the quotient of \(G\) by the closed invariant subgroup of \(G\) generated by the \(\overline{g}_i\).

When \(a'\) is invariant under \(\Gamma\), one sees easily that \(\Gamma\) operates naturally on \(\pi_1(S',a')\), and \(G\) is identified with the corresponding semi-direct product. Identifying then \(\Gamma\) with a subgroup of \(G\), one sees that in the relations introduced above, taking \(b'=a'\), one finds “ \(g = e\) ” for \(g\in I\). Hence if \(S'\) has a geometric point \(a'\) fixed by \(I\) (i.e. a point \(s'\) whose inertia group is \(\Gamma\)), then \(\pi_1(S,a)\) is a quotient group of the quotient group of Galois type of \(\pi_1(S',a')\) obtained by “ making trivial ” the operations of \(\Gamma\) on \(\pi_1(S',a')\); and it is even isomorphic to this last group if one supposes that for every \(g\in G\), the inertia set \(S_g'\) is connected, hence passes through the locality of \(a'\). This last assertion is indeed contained in the second description given above for the relations to be introduced in \(G\).

This last result applies in particular if one takes for \(S'\) the cartesian power \(X^n\) of a connected prescheme over an algebraically closed field, for \(\Gamma\) the symmetric group \(\Gamma={\mathfrak{S}}_n\), operating in the usual way, whence for \(S\) the symmetric power of \(S\). Taking then for \(a'\) a geometric point localized at the diagonal, one is under the conditions above, the inertia sets \(S_g'\) indeed all containing the diagonal. Using the fact, proved in the following exposé, that if \(X\) is proper connected over \(k\), the fundamental group of \(X^n\) is identified with \(\pi_1(X)^n\), one finds the following amusing result: If \(X\) is proper connected over \(k\) algebraically closed, the fundamental group of its symmetric power , \(n \geqslant 2\), is isomorphic to the fundamental group of \(X\) made abelian. (I do not know whether the analogous fact in algebraic Topology is known; it should be possible to establish it by the same method of descent). Taking for example for \(X\) a rational curve \(X=\mathbf{P}^1_k\), one finds a proof of the fact that \(\mathbf{P}^r_k\) is simply connected, using the fact that \(\mathbf{P}^1_k\) is so. Let us now take for \(X\) a simple curve over \(k\), and \(n\geqslant 2g-1\), so that \(\operatorname{Symm}^n(X)\) is fibred over the jacobian \(J\), with fibres projective spaces, hence (as one will see with the aid of the results of the two following exposés) has the same fundamental group as \(J\). One then recovers without dévissage the well-known fact that the fundamental group of the jacobian of \(X\) is isomorphic to the fundamental group of \(X\) made abelian.

footnotes.html: One should take care that the element \(\overline{s'}\) whose existence is implicitly admitted does not exist in all cases. Consequently, the theorem 1 as it is stated does not apply in all cases. It is not difficult, however, by taking inspiration from the written text, to modify the statement of this theorem in such a way that it gives a method of computation which applies in all cases. In particular, the corollaries of the said theorem are valid as they stand.

This seems
very improbable in the case of smooth curves of genus \(g\geqslant 2\),
in characteristic \(p>0\). When one replaces \(\pi_1\) by its largest
quotient prime to \(p\), on the other hand, it seems that the
well-known techniques allow one to give an affirmative
answer, even without any properness hypothesis. Cf. a
work in preparation by J.P.  Murre.

pages.title:
A fundamental exact sequence. Descent by morphisms with
relatively connected fibres
pages.html:
6 A fundamental exact sequence. Descent by morphisms with relatively connected fibres

Theorem 1

Let \(S\) be the spectrum of an Artinian ring \(A\) with residue field \(k\), \(\overline{k}\) an algebraic closure of \(k\), \(X\) an \(S\) prescheme, \(X_0=X\otimes_A k\), \(\overline{X}_0=X\otimes_A \overline{k}\), \(\overline{a}\) a geometric point of \(\overline{X}\), \(a\) its image in \(X\), \(b\) its image in \(S\). Suppose that \(X_0\) is quasi-compact and geometrically connected over \(k\) (N.B. if \(X\) is proper over \(S\), this means that \(\mathrm{H}^0(X_0,\mathscr{O}_{X_0})\) is a local Artinian ring with residue field radicial over \(k\)). Then the sequence of canonical homomorphisms

\[ e\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \pi_1(\overline{X}_0,\overline{a})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(S,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e \]

is exact, and we have

\[ \pi_1(S,b)\overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} \pi_1(k,\overline{k})=\text{Galois group of~$\overline{k}$ over~$k$.} \]

Since the fundamental groups do not change upon cutting out by the nilpotent elements, we may suppose \(A=k\), which already renders the last isomorphism evident. Let \(k'\) be the separable closure of \(k\) in \(\overline{k}\), and consider \(X'=X\otimes_k k'\), and the image \(a'\) of \(\overline{a}\) in \(X'\). We have a sequence of canonical homomorphisms

\[ e\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \pi_1(\overline{X}_0,\overline{a})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X',a')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(S',b')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e \]

(where \(S'=\operatorname{Spec}(k')\)). Finally, we have a canonical homomorphism from this sequence into the one relative to \(X/k\), thanks to the diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{S}&{X}\ar[l]&{\overline{X}_0}\ar[l]\\{S'}\ar[u]&{X'}\ar[u]\ar[l]&{\overline{X}_0 .}\ar[l]\ar@{=}[u]} \]

On the other hand we see that this homomorphism of sequences of groups is an isomorphism, as follows from 11. We are thus reduced to proving that the second sequence is exact, i.e. we may suppose that \(k\) is perfect. Let then \(k_i\) be the finite Galois subextensions of \(k\) in \(\overline{k}\), set \(X_i=X\otimes_k k_i\), and let \(a_i\) be the image of \(\overline{a}\) in \(X_i\). We leave to the reader the task of verifying that the natural homomorphism

\[ \pi_1(\overline{X}_0,a)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \varprojlim\pi_1(X_i,a_i) \]

is an isomorphism, which simply means that an étale covering of \(\overline{X}\) comes from an étale covering of some \(X_i\), and that the latter is essentially unique, modulo passage to some \(X_j\), \(j\geqslant i\). On the other hand, let \(\pi_i\) be the Galois group of \(k_i\) over \(k\), i.e. the group opposite to the group of \(S\)-automorphisms of \(S_i=\operatorname{Spec}(k_i)\). Since the functor \(S'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X\times_S S'\) from étale coverings of \(S\) into the étale coverings of \(X\) is fully faithful (4), it follows that \(\pi_i\) is also isomorphic to the group opposite to the groups of \(X\)-automorphisms of the connected principal covering \(X_i\) of \(X\). It therefore follows from V 13 that we have an exact sequence

\[ e\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \pi_1(X_i,a_i)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e. \]

Passing to the inverse limit over \(i\) in these exact sequences, we find an exact sequence (since we are in the category of groups of Galois type), which is none other than the sequence considered in 1. This completes the proof.

The translation of the exactness on the right in 1 into geometric language is the following:

Corollary 2

With the preceding notation, let \(X'\) be an étale covering of \(X\), and let \(\overline{X'}_0\) be the corresponding étale covering of \(\overline{X}_0\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    There exists an \(S'\) étale over \(S\), and an \(X\)-isomorphism \(X'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X\times_S S'\), (\(S'\) is then determined up to unique isomorphism by virtue of 4).

  2. (ii)

    \(\overline{X}_0'\) is completely decomposed over \(\overline{X}_0\).

    If \(X'\) is connected, these conditions are also equivalent to:

  3. (ii bis)

    \(\overline{X}_0'\) has a section over \(\overline{X}_0\).

(N.B. This last complement is essential; the equivalence of (i) and (ii) means only that \(\pi_1(S,b)\) is the quotient group of \(\pi_1(X,a)\) by the closed invariant subgroup generated by the image of \(\pi_1(\overline{X}_0,\overline{a})\), and not by this image itself). Under the preceding conditions, we shall say that \(X'\) is a geometrically trivial covering of \(X\).

Remark 3

In statement 1 one cannot replace \(\overline{k}\) by an arbitrary algebraically closed extension of \(k\), even if \(k\) is already supposed algebraically closed. In other words, it is not true in general that if \(X\) is a connected algebraic scheme over an algebraically closed field \(k\), its fundamental group does not change upon replacing \(k\) by an algebraically closed extension; this is already false for example in characteristic \(p>0\) for the affine line over \(k\), because of the phenomena of “higher ramification” at the point at infinity, implying a “continuous” structure for the fundamental group. We shall see however in the following exposé that such phenomena cannot occur if \(X\) is proper over \(k\). We shall also show by transcendental means that the same holds if \(k\) is of characteristic zero.

Corollary 4

Suppose that \(a\) is localized at an \(x\in X\) which is rational over \(k\) (or more generally, having a residue field radicial over \(k\)). Then the exact sequence 1 is split.

We may suppose \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(k)\). If \(x\) is rational over \(k\), it corresponds to a section \(S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) of \(X\) over \(S\), transforming \(b\) into \(a\), and defining a homomorphism \(\pi_1(S,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\) which is the sought-for splitting. If \(\mathit{k}(x)\) is radicial over \(k\), we reduce to the preceding case by making the base extension \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathit{k}(x))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Spec}(k)\).

Theorem 5

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper and surjective morphism of finite presentation, with geometrically connected fibres, \(X'\) a prescheme of finite presentation and proper over \(X\), \(s\) a point of \(S\), \(F=X_s\) the fibre of \(X\) at \(s\), and \(F_1'\) a connected component of the fibre \(F'=X'_s\) of \(X'\) at \(s\). In order for there to exist an open neighbourhood \(X_1'\) of \(F_1'\) in \(X'\), an étale \(S\)-scheme \(S_1'\) and an \(X\)-isomorphism \(X_1'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} S_1'\times_S X\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(X'\) be étale over \(X\) at the points of \(F_1'\), and that \(F_1'\) be a geometrically trivial covering of \(F\).

The necessity of the condition being trivial, it remains to prove the sufficiency. We reduce easily to the case where \(S\) is Noetherian. Consider the Stein factorization \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) of \(f\), where \(T\) is the spectrum of the Algebra \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)\) over \(S\). Since the fibres of \(X\) over \(S\) are geometrically connected, and \(f\) is surjective, the morphism \(T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is finite surjective and radicial, hence (10) every \(T'\) étale over \(T\) comes by inverse image from an \(S'\) étale over \(S\). This reduces us to proving 5 in it replacing \(S\) by \(T\), i.e. in the case where one supposes \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)=\mathscr{O}_S\). Consider then the Stein factorization \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) of the proper morphism \(h\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), where \(S'\) is the spectrum of the Algebra \(h_*(\mathscr{O}_{X'})\). The morphisms \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) define a canonical morphism

\[ X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_S S', \]

and our assertion is contained in the following one:

Corollary 6

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper morphism of locally Noetherian preschemes, such that \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)=\mathscr{O}_S\), and let \(X'\) be a prescheme proper over \(X\). Consider the Stein factorization \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) for \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), and the canonical morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_S S'\). Let \(s\) be a point of \(S\), \(s'\) a point of \(S'\) above \(s\), corresponding to a connected component \(F_1'\) of the fibre \(X'_s\) of \(X'\) at \(s\). In order that the morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_S S'\) be an isomorphism above an open neighbourhood \(U'\) of \(s'\) étale over \(S\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(X'\) be étale over \(X\) at the points of \(F_1'\), and that \(F_1'\) be a geometrically trivial covering of the fibre \(F=X_s\).

The necessity being again trivial, it remains to prove the sufficiency. The conclusion also means that a) the morphism deduced from \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_S S'\) by the base change \(\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{s'})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) is an isomorphism, b) \(S'\) is étale over \(S\) at \(s'\), i.e. \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{s'}\) is étale over \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_s\). In this form, one sees that the conclusion is invariant under the base change \(\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_s)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). Since the hypotheses are likewise stable under this base change, we may therefore assume that \(S\) is the spectrum of a complete Noetherian local ring. We may moreover evidently assume \(X'\) connected, which here implies that \(S'=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{s'})\), \(F'=F_1'\).

Since the set of points of \(X'\) where \(X'\) is étale over \(X\) is open and contains the fibre \(X'_{s'}=F'\), \(X'\) being proper over \(S\), it follows that \(X'\) is étale over \(X\). Since it induces on \(F=X_s\) an étale covering isomorphic to a \(F\otimes_{\mathit{k}(s)}L\), where \(L\) is étale over \(\mathit{k}(s)\), it follows from 10 that it is isomorphic to a covering of the form \(X\times_S T\), with \(T\) étale over \(S\). (N.B. here again, it suffices to use that the functor of 10 is fully faithful, which follows from the fact that a formal isomorphism of coherent sheaves on \(X\) comes from an isomorphism of these sheaves). Hence, if \(T\) is defined by the algebra \(B\) finite over \(A\), \(X'\) is identified with the spectrum of the Algebra \(\mathscr{O}_X\otimes_A B\) over \(X\), whence it follows at once, since \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)=\mathscr{O}_S\), that \(h_*(\mathscr{O}_{X'})\) is defined by \(B\), so the canonical homomorphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_S S'\) is none other than the isomorphism under consideration \(X'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X\times_S T\). This completes the proof.

Corollary 7

Under the conditions of 5, in order that there exist a prescheme \(S'\) étale over \(S\) and an \(X\)-isomorphism \(X'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X\times_S S'\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(X'\) be étale over \(X\) and that for every \(s\in S\), \(X'_{s'}\) be a geometrically trivial covering of \(X_s\).

Indeed, if this is so, \(X'\) is a union of open sets \(X_i'\) which are isomorphic to inverse images of \(S_i'\) étale over \(S\). One then sees easily that these \(S_i'\) glue together into an \(S'\) étale over \(S\), and that one obtains an isomorphism \(X'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X\times_S S'\). One can say for example that the \(X_i'\) are equipped with descent data relative to \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), which glue together necessarily into a descent datum on the whole of \(X'\) relative to \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). And since the latter is effective on the \(X_i'\), it follows easily (thanks to a sorite forgotten in section 4) that it is effective. One can also state 7 in the following form:

Corollary 8

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper surjective morphism of finite presentation, with geometrically connected fibres. Then \(f\) is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of preschemes étale and finite over others. The functor \(S'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X\times_S S'\) induces an equivalence of the category of preschemes étale and finite over \(S\) with the category of preschemes étale and finite over \(X\) which induce on each fibre \(X_s\) a geometrically trivial covering.

Remark 9

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper and surjective morphism, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian; then \(f\) factors into a morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) satisfying the hypothesis of 8, and a finite surjective morphism \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) amenable to 7, hence into a product of two morphisms which are universal morphisms of effective descent for the fibred category of preschemes étale and finite over others. One can from this conclude that \(f\) itself is a morphism of universal effective descent for the fibred category under consideration. One thus recovers 12 by a different method.

Remark 10

The conclusion of 7 does not remain valid if one replaces the hypothesis that \(f\) is proper by: \(X\) is of finite type over \(S\) and admits a section over \(S\) (so that \(f\) is universally submersive and a morphism of descent for the fibred category of preschemes étale over others), even when \(S\) is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring and when \(X'\) is an étale covering of \(X\). To see this, one starts from a \(Z\) proper over \(S\), whose general fibre is a non-singular rational curve, and whose special fibre \(Z_0\) consists of two concurrent lines. For example, if \(t\) is a uniformizer of the valuation ring \(A\), one takes the closed subscheme \(Z\) of \(\mathbf{P}_A^2\) defined by the homogeneous equation \(x^2+y^2+tz^2=0\) (homogeneous coordinates \(x\), \(y\), \(z\)). One takes for \(X\) the complement of the singular point \(a\) of \(Z_0\) in the union \(Z\cup \mathbf{P}_k^2\). The fibres of \(X\) are \(\mathbf{P}_k^1\) and \(\mathbf{P}_k^2 - a\), and are therefore geometrically simply connected (i.e. every étale covering of such a fibre is geometrically trivial). Nevertheless one constructs easily, proceeding as in 4, some étale coverings of \(X\) which do not come from étale coverings of \(S\), by gluing trivial coverings of \(Z-a\) and of \(\mathbf{P}_k^2-a\). It is possible, on the other hand, that the conclusion of 7 subsists if one replaces in it the hypothesis of properness by that of \(X\) being universally open of finite presentation over \(S\)1. This is true at least if one assumes that the fibres of \(X\) over \(S\) are geometrically irreducible, and not merely geometrically connected. Let us only point out that in this question one can reduce to the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring with algebraically closed residue field.

The interpretation of 7 in terms of the fundamental group is the following:

Corollary 11

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper surjective morphism of finite presentation, with geometrically connected fibres. Suppose \(X\), hence \(S\), connected. Let \(a\) be a geometric point of \(X\), \(b\) its image in \(S\), and for every \(s\in S\), let us choose an algebraic closure \(\overline{\mathit{k}(s)}\) of \(\mathit{k}(s)\), a geometric point \(a_s\) of \(S_s\) with values in this extension, and a class of paths from \(a_s\) to \(a\), whence a homomorphism \(\pi_1(\overline X_s,a_s)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\), where \(\overline X_s=X_s\otimes_{\mathit{k}(s)}\overline{\mathit{k}(s)}\). Then the homomorphism \(\pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(S,b)\) is surjective, and its kernel is the closed invariant subgroup of \(\pi_1(X,a)\) generated by the images of the \(\pi_1(\overline X_s,a_s)\).

Remark 12

Under the conditions of 7, assuming \(S\) Noetherian, one sees easily that the set of points \(s\in S\) such that \(S'_{s'}\) is geometrically trivial over \(X_s\) is a constructible set; if \(X'\) is proper over \(X\), it is even open, as one sees from 6. This therefore allows, if \(S\) is a Jacobson prescheme (for example of finite type over a field), or \(X'\) is proper over \(X\), to restrict oneself, in order to verify the conditions of 7, to the points \(s\) of \(S\) that are closed. Like- wise, in 11 it then suffices to take the \(\pi_1(\overline X_s,a_s)\) for the points of \(S\) that are closed.

[D]
J. Giraud, Méthode de la descente, Mémoire 2 de la Soc. Math. Fr., 1964. ↩
[1]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1

Let \(S\) be the spectrum of an Artinian ring \(A\) with residue field \(k\), \(\overline{k}\) an algebraic closure of \(k\), \(X\) an \(S\) prescheme, \(X_0=X\otimes_A k\), \(\overline{X}_0=X\otimes_A \overline{k}\), \(\overline{a}\) a geometric point of \(\overline{X}\), \(a\) its image in \(X\), \(b\) its image in \(S\). Suppose that \(X_0\) is quasi-compact and geometrically connected over \(k\) (N.B. if \(X\) is proper over \(S\), this means that \(\mathrm{H}^0(X_0,\mathscr{O}_{X_0})\) is a local Artinian ring with residue field radicial over \(k\)). Then the sequence of canonical homomorphisms

\[ e\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \pi_1(\overline{X}_0,\overline{a})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(S,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e \]

is exact, and we have

\[ \pi_1(S,b)\overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} \pi_1(k,\overline{k})=\text{Galois group of~$\overline{k}$ over~$k$.} \]

Since the fundamental groups do not change upon cutting out by the nilpotent elements, we may suppose \(A=k\), which already renders the last isomorphism evident. Let \(k'\) be the separable closure of \(k\) in \(\overline{k}\), and consider \(X'=X\otimes_k k'\), and the image \(a'\) of \(\overline{a}\) in \(X'\). We have a sequence of canonical homomorphisms

\[ e\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \pi_1(\overline{X}_0,\overline{a})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X',a')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(S',b')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e \]
(where \(S'=\operatorname{Spec}(k')\)). Finally, we have a canonical homomorphism from this sequence into the one relative to \(X/k\), thanks to the diagram
\[ \xymatrix{{S}&{X}\ar[l]&{\overline{X}_0}\ar[l]\\{S'}\ar[u]&{X'}\ar[u]\ar[l]&{\overline{X}_0 .}\ar[l]\ar@{=}[u]} \]

On the other hand we see that this homomorphism of sequences of groups is an isomorphism, as follows from 11. We are thus reduced to proving that the second sequence is exact, i.e. we may suppose that \(k\) is perfect. Let then \(k_i\) be the finite Galois subextensions of \(k\) in \(\overline{k}\), set \(X_i=X\otimes_k k_i\), and let \(a_i\) be the image of \(\overline{a}\) in \(X_i\). We leave to the reader the task of verifying that the natural homomorphism
\[ \pi_1(\overline{X}_0,a)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \varprojlim\pi_1(X_i,a_i) \]

is an isomorphism, which simply means that an étale covering of \(\overline{X}\) comes from an étale covering of some \(X_i\), and that the latter is essentially unique, modulo passage to some \(X_j\), \(j\geqslant i\). On the other hand, let \(\pi_i\) be the Galois group of \(k_i\) over \(k\), i.e. the group opposite to the group of \(S\)-automorphisms of \(S_i=\operatorname{Spec}(k_i)\). Since the functor \(S'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X\times_S S'\) from étale coverings of \(S\) into the étale coverings of \(X\) is fully faithful (4), it follows that \(\pi_i\) is also isomorphic to the group opposite to the groups of \(X\)-automorphisms of the connected principal covering \(X_i\) of \(X\). It therefore follows from V 13 that we have an exact sequence

\[ e\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \pi_1(X_i,a_i)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e. \]

Passing to the inverse limit over \(i\) in these exact sequences, we find an exact sequence (since we are in the category of groups of Galois type), which is none other than the sequence considered in 1. This completes the proof.
The translation of the exactness on the right in 1 into geometric language is the following:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

With the preceding notation, let \(X'\) be an étale covering of \(X\), and let \(\overline{X'}_0\) be the corresponding étale covering of \(\overline{X}_0\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    There exists an \(S'\) étale over \(S\), and an \(X\)-isomorphism \(X'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X\times_S S'\), (\(S'\) is then determined up to unique isomorphism by virtue of 4).

  2. (ii)

    \(\overline{X}_0'\) is completely decomposed over \(\overline{X}_0\).

    If \(X'\) is connected, these conditions are also equivalent to:

  3. (ii bis)

    \(\overline{X}_0'\) has a section over \(\overline{X}_0\).

(N.B. This last complement is essential; the equivalence of (i) and (ii) means only that \(\pi_1(S,b)\) is the quotient group of \(\pi_1(X,a)\) by the closed invariant subgroup generated by the image of \(\pi_1(\overline{X}_0,\overline{a})\), and not by this image itself). Under the preceding conditions, we shall say that \(X'\) is a geometrically trivial covering of \(X\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3

In statement 1 one cannot replace \(\overline{k}\) by an arbitrary algebraically closed extension of \(k\), even if \(k\) is already supposed algebraically closed. In other words, it is not true in general that if \(X\) is a connected algebraic scheme over an algebraically closed field \(k\), its fundamental group does not change upon replacing \(k\) by an algebraically closed extension; this is already false for example in characteristic \(p>0\) for the affine line over \(k\), because of the phenomena of “higher ramification” at the point at infinity, implying a “continuous” structure for the fundamental group. We shall see however in the following exposé that such phenomena cannot occur if \(X\) is proper over \(k\). We shall also show by transcendental means that the same holds if \(k\) is of characteristic zero.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

Suppose that \(a\) is localized at an \(x\in X\) which is rational over \(k\) (or more generally, having a residue field radicial over \(k\)). Then the exact sequence 1 is split.

We may suppose \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(k)\). If \(x\) is rational over \(k\), it corresponds to a section \(S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) of \(X\) over \(S\), transforming \(b\) into \(a\), and defining a homomorphism \(\pi_1(S,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\) which is the sought-for splitting. If \(\mathit{k}(x)\) is radicial over \(k\), we reduce to the preceding case by making the base extension \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathit{k}(x))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Spec}(k)\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper and surjective morphism of finite presentation, with geometrically connected fibres, \(X'\) a prescheme of finite presentation and proper over \(X\), \(s\) a point of \(S\), \(F=X_s\) the fibre of \(X\) at \(s\), and \(F_1'\) a connected component of the fibre \(F'=X'_s\) of \(X'\) at \(s\). In order for there to exist an open neighbourhood \(X_1'\) of \(F_1'\) in \(X'\), an étale \(S\)-scheme \(S_1'\) and an \(X\)-isomorphism \(X_1'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} S_1'\times_S X\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(X'\) be étale over \(X\) at the points of \(F_1'\), and that \(F_1'\) be a geometrically trivial covering of \(F\).

The necessity of the condition being trivial, it remains to prove the sufficiency. We reduce easily to the case where \(S\) is Noetherian. Consider the Stein factorization \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) of \(f\), where \(T\) is the spectrum of the Algebra \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)\) over \(S\). Since the fibres of \(X\) over \(S\) are geometrically connected, and \(f\) is surjective, the morphism \(T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is finite surjective and radicial, hence (10) every \(T'\) étale over \(T\) comes by inverse image from an \(S'\) étale over \(S\). This reduces us to proving 5 in it replacing \(S\) by \(T\), i.e. in the case where one supposes \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)=\mathscr{O}_S\). Consider then the Stein factorization \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) of the proper morphism \(h\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), where \(S'\) is the spectrum of the Algebra \(h_*(\mathscr{O}_{X'})\). The morphisms \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) define a canonical morphism
\[ X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_S S', \]
and our assertion is contained in the following one:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper morphism of locally Noetherian preschemes, such that \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)=\mathscr{O}_S\), and let \(X'\) be a prescheme proper over \(X\). Consider the Stein factorization \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) for \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), and the canonical morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_S S'\). Let \(s\) be a point of \(S\), \(s'\) a point of \(S'\) above \(s\), corresponding to a connected component \(F_1'\) of the fibre \(X'_s\) of \(X'\) at \(s\). In order that the morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_S S'\) be an isomorphism above an open neighbourhood \(U'\) of \(s'\) étale over \(S\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(X'\) be étale over \(X\) at the points of \(F_1'\), and that \(F_1'\) be a geometrically trivial covering of the fibre \(F=X_s\).

The necessity being again trivial, it remains to prove the sufficiency. The conclusion also means that a) the morphism deduced from \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_S S'\) by the base change \(\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{s'})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) is an isomorphism, b) \(S'\) is étale over \(S\) at \(s'\), i.e. \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{s'}\) is étale over \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_s\). In this form, one sees that the conclusion is invariant under the base change \(\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_s)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). Since the hypotheses are likewise stable under this base change, we may therefore assume that \(S\) is the spectrum of a complete Noetherian local ring. We may moreover evidently assume \(X'\) connected, which here implies that \(S'=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{s'})\), \(F'=F_1'\).

Since the set of points of \(X'\) where \(X'\) is étale over \(X\) is open and contains the fibre \(X'_{s'}=F'\), \(X'\) being proper over \(S\), it follows that \(X'\) is étale over \(X\). Since it induces on \(F=X_s\) an étale covering isomorphic to a \(F\otimes_{\mathit{k}(s)}L\), where \(L\) is étale over \(\mathit{k}(s)\), it follows from 10 that it is isomorphic to a covering of the form \(X\times_S T\), with \(T\) étale over \(S\). (N.B. here again, it suffices to use that the functor of 10 is fully faithful, which follows from the fact that a formal isomorphism of coherent sheaves on \(X\) comes from an isomorphism of these sheaves). Hence, if \(T\) is defined by the algebra \(B\) finite over \(A\), \(X'\) is identified with the spectrum of the Algebra \(\mathscr{O}_X\otimes_A B\) over \(X\), whence it follows at once, since \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)=\mathscr{O}_S\), that \(h_*(\mathscr{O}_{X'})\) is defined by \(B\), so the canonical homomorphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_S S'\) is none other than the isomorphism under consideration \(X'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X\times_S T\). This completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7

Under the conditions of 5, in order that there exist a prescheme \(S'\) étale over \(S\) and an \(X\)-isomorphism \(X'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X\times_S S'\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(X'\) be étale over \(X\) and that for every \(s\in S\), \(X'_{s'}\) be a geometrically trivial covering of \(X_s\).

Indeed, if this is so, \(X'\) is a union of open sets \(X_i'\) which are isomorphic to inverse images of \(S_i'\) étale over \(S\). One then sees easily that these \(S_i'\) glue together into an \(S'\) étale over \(S\), and that one obtains an isomorphism \(X'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X\times_S S'\). One can say for example that the \(X_i'\) are equipped with descent data relative to \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), which glue together necessarily into a descent datum on the whole of \(X'\) relative to \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). And since the latter is effective on the \(X_i'\), it follows easily (thanks to a sorite forgotten in section 4) that it is effective. One can also state 7 in the following form:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper surjective morphism of finite presentation, with geometrically connected fibres. Then \(f\) is a morphism of effective descent for the fibred category of preschemes étale and finite over others. The functor \(S'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X\times_S S'\) induces an equivalence of the category of preschemes étale and finite over \(S\) with the category of preschemes étale and finite over \(X\) which induce on each fibre \(X_s\) a geometrically trivial covering.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 9

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper and surjective morphism, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian; then \(f\) factors into a morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) satisfying the hypothesis of 8, and a finite surjective morphism \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) amenable to 7, hence into a product of two morphisms which are universal morphisms of effective descent for the fibred category of preschemes étale and finite over others. One can from this conclude that \(f\) itself is a morphism of universal effective descent for the fibred category under consideration. One thus recovers 12 by a different method.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 10

The conclusion of 7 does not remain valid if one replaces the hypothesis that \(f\) is proper by: \(X\) is of finite type over \(S\) and admits a section over \(S\) (so that \(f\) is universally submersive and a morphism of descent for the fibred category of preschemes étale over others), even when \(S\) is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring and when \(X'\) is an étale covering of \(X\). To see this, one starts from a \(Z\) proper over \(S\), whose general fibre is a non-singular rational curve, and whose special fibre \(Z_0\) consists of two concurrent lines. For example, if \(t\) is a uniformizer of the valuation ring \(A\), one takes the closed subscheme \(Z\) of \(\mathbf{P}_A^2\) defined by the homogeneous equation \(x^2+y^2+tz^2=0\) (homogeneous coordinates \(x\), \(y\), \(z\)). One takes for \(X\) the complement of the singular point \(a\) of \(Z_0\) in the union \(Z\cup \mathbf{P}_k^2\). The fibres of \(X\) are \(\mathbf{P}_k^1\) and \(\mathbf{P}_k^2 - a\), and are therefore geometrically simply connected (i.e. every étale covering of such a fibre is geometrically trivial). Nevertheless one constructs easily, proceeding as in 4, some étale coverings of \(X\) which do not come from étale coverings of \(S\), by gluing trivial coverings of \(Z-a\) and of \(\mathbf{P}_k^2-a\). It is possible, on the other hand, that the conclusion of 7 subsists if one replaces in it the hypothesis of properness by that of \(X\) being universally open of finite presentation over \(S\)1. This is true at least if one assumes that the fibres of \(X\) over \(S\) are geometrically irreducible, and not merely geometrically connected. Let us only point out that in this question one can reduce to the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring with algebraically closed residue field.
The interpretation of 7 in terms of the fundamental group is the following:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 11

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a proper surjective morphism of finite presentation, with geometrically connected fibres. Suppose \(X\), hence \(S\), connected. Let \(a\) be a geometric point of \(X\), \(b\) its image in \(S\), and for every \(s\in S\), let us choose an algebraic closure \(\overline{\mathit{k}(s)}\) of \(\mathit{k}(s)\), a geometric point \(a_s\) of \(S_s\) with values in this extension, and a class of paths from \(a_s\) to \(a\), whence a homomorphism \(\pi_1(\overline X_s,a_s)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\), where \(\overline X_s=X_s\otimes_{\mathit{k}(s)}\overline{\mathit{k}(s)}\). Then the homomorphism \(\pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(S,b)\) is surjective, and its kernel is the closed invariant subgroup of \(\pi_1(X,a)\) generated by the images of the \(\pi_1(\overline X_s,a_s)\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 12

Under the conditions of 7, assuming \(S\) Noetherian, one sees easily that the set of points \(s\in S\) such that \(S'_{s'}\) is geometrically trivial over \(X_s\) is a constructible set; if \(X'\) is proper over \(X\), it is even open, as one sees from 6. This therefore allows, if \(S\) is a Jacobson prescheme (for example of finite type over a field), or \(X'\) is proper over \(X\), to restrict oneself, in order to verify the conditions of 7, to the points \(s\) of \(S\) that are closed. Like- wise, in 11 it then suffices to take the \(\pi_1(\overline X_s,a_s)\) for the points of \(S\) that are closed.
